author: Jim Stutts
text: My dad John road a VL in the 30´s and 40´s with the Victor Mc Laglen Motor Corps 21 Man Stunt and Drill Team out of Los Angeles. I don´t know how old I was, but my first taste of speed came from that VL as daddy went through the gears with that side shifter. Being the largest guy in the VMMC, his bike became the vehicle to carry all 21 around the LA Colosseum and other stadiums in their shows, not to mention 4 way cross-overs at 50 mph, seat standing, etc. Dad road his VL from LA to Charlotte, NC and back, by himself! Sadly, his riding days ended when a collision with a car, penned his left leg between the crash bar and the engine, crippling him badly for the rest of his life. But his love for Harley´s never stopped! I thought someone out there might like this memory of mine!

author: Iain Beck
text: Hello fellow 64XLCH owners, this is my first post and probably not the last. Can any one tell me what the ratio is for the speedo ie one to one or two to one and also where I can get an original speedo I live in the uk and cannot get a speedo over here......cheers

author: Tom Benouli
text: I wish I could help you. My 64 XLCH did not come with a speedo, tach, odometer, or mirrors. I believe that in 1964 *electric signals* were optional!  Oh, those were the days. The original 1964 Sportsters only came with a speedo and no other instrumentation. You can make a variety of speedos work but if you are looking for original equipment then you have an adventure ahead of you. I´d keep an eye on Ebay to see if one pops up. If you are trying to configure the speedo in specific integral ratios (i.e. one to one or two to one, but not one to 2.3, etc.) then I´d just install it and ride around. If it is obviously wrong then change the ratio.

My friend sends him lots of business so he just threw it out there that he would go over the whole bike for very little money!! I was shocked! It´ll be like Christmas! Wires, bolts, cables, fluids. text: So I bought my 1970 Harley XLCH Sportster in the summer of 1992 and it was still in relatively great shape. Ran like a dream and I rode the hell out of it for 4 years until it got too expensive for a 22 year old with small kids making $9 bucks an hour to afford along with a \

author: Mac11b
text: Yea, its an older bike.  It may be a starter bike.  It is not the fastest or prettiest bike ever made, but it will get you where you want to go whether it is to the local pub or accross the the country and back.  I have tweeked mine a bit and will continue to do so.  At a minimum you need to upgrade the suspension and the seat.  After that it is all about personality and you can go in just about any direction with this blank slate.  I have put almost 30K mi. in the 4 years I have owned mine.  I did an Iron Butt 1K/24, ridden to Sturgis and have other grand plans.  You won´t see alot of older Sporties, but if you ride one you will be hard pressed to ever ride a better one.  It is a great bike to learn how to turn a wrench on.  I am no mechanic, but I have learned alot in 4 yrs.  You can get them cheap and usually with low miles, then ride it like you stole it and the smile will never leave your face.

author: pjb
text: After not owning a bike for 25 years I bought a 93 Sportster 883 Hugger. I was looking for something easy to ride and with enough power to travel on the interstate if necessary. I just returned from a 500 mile trip that was almost spiritual in nature and have truely bonded with the bike. If you´re looking for a great all round bike you won´t be disappointed with a Sportster. The 883 purred along the whole way and got an average of 58 mpg. The power is adequate for a single rider and gear. I prefered to travel on the two lane state routes which afford you a glimpse of the small towns you have to slow down to pass through and 55 mph is a real nice cruising speed for this bike. I did have to take the interstate for a while and got blown around pretty badly by the 18 wheelers. If I planned on a trip primarily on the interstate I´d surely want a Road King. But small bikes have their place too and the Sportster is great around town and easy to get turned around in a small space. Also great for short riders who want to plant their feet at the stops. The stock seat was a little hard 150 miles into the trip and I´ll probably upgrade that before the next one. The gas tank is small and I needed to stop every 80-100 miles but that was actually a pleasure to get off the bike and stretch every hour or two. All in all I love this bike and would recommend it to anyone. It may be considered a girls bike by some but I see it as an economical and very maneuverable bike that´s a blast to ride and I´m glad I bought one.

author: hillclimber
text: it´s AMF

author: GeF
text: AMF bought Harley Davidson in 1973. So the 1971 Sportster is not an AMF!

author: emej
text: AMF purchased HD, however they still manufactured the XLH/XLCH using the HD produced parts still available before the company had switched hands. They didn´t run out of the original HD parts until 72, at which time they had to depend on their own manufacturing techniques. The 71 was put out by AMF, but is still truly an HD motorcycle.

author: Izzy-c
text: Hi!  I´m a new rider, having just completed the Basic Rider´s Course at my local Harley dealer, and I´ve always heard that the Sportster is not the best bike for people to learn on.  While I agree, it´s not nearly as bad as everyone told me it would be.  The best advice I received was from a fellow Sportster rider who told me to stick with it and that in a relatively short time, the bike would become second nature, a part of me.  He was right.  I´ve spent the last month "terrorizing" the neighborhood - riding our streets up and down, getting used to the feel, the power, the handling.  Today was the first day I decided "ok, enough - time to get out on the main streets."  What a rush.  The bike itself is a little rough and does have a lot of power - especially for those of us just learning.  I highly recommend anyone wanting to learn to take a riders course.  It made all the difference in the world to me - I know I can handle the curves, turns and corners and it is all due to being able to learn with able, experienced teachers in a controlled atmosphere.  The best part - I can go back anytime and practice on their bikes or bring mine.  I really can´t think of any negatives for this bike, except maybe that it´s a little small to handle two people (my husband rides, and it´s a little crowded to have both of us on it).  Great bike for around town and for day trips - definitely not a touring bike, though.  I highly recommend the Hugger to anyone interested - great bike for the money!

author: NN
text: I have a 71 Sportster right-hand. Would like to change to left side. Was told 1975-76 were changed. Is it possible to change? Using parts from 75-76?

author: piniongear
text: The changeover was made in 1975 due to Federal regulation, calling for all shifting to be on the same side, i.e.,leftside on motorcycles manufactured after that year. Harley started with the shifter shaft still coming out the timing cover (on the rightside) and then ran linkage thru the frame to connect with the shift lever on the primary side. It was a pile of garbage, serving no purpose, other than to meet the Feds dictate. I also own a 1971 XLCH, bought it 30 years ago. Ride it every day. Now, I have to ask, why in the world would you want to tarnish such a fine machine by putting garbage like that on it? Maybe you are a newby who has never ridden anything before that shifts on the right? Forget about doing the changeover. You could never find the parts needed to do it anyway.

author: NN
text: My R60/6, when running in the worst of shape was around 22 mpg.  After new rings and a good carb cleaning I also hit about 30.  However with 100 octane low lead aviation fuel, I was easily hitting 40 with an abundant increase in power.  Using BMW´s oil in the recomended viscosity seems to help overall performance and economy.  Also the entire charging and ignition system is very important to maintain as meticulously as possible.  Test it, replace what may be worn, then clean it´s components behind the front cover with non alcohol cleaner as often as needed via visual inspection.  Good Luck.

How about a 1976 R90/6? And should I be looking for just a couple of bodies, or is it too much work for the price difference of replacing the whole carbs? The carbs on the bike are the stock bings. author: NN
text: This is a lovely bike. Not super fast but loads of torque and inherently reliable. While I never owned one, I put many thousands of kilometres on a short wheelbase model belonging to my brother-in-law. THis was an ex-police bike which he bought sight unseen with 12,000 miles on it. I picked it up the day it came into the shop from the police department and rode it from Melbourne to Adelaide 2 days later (700k). The police fairing had too much drag so that came off & an "S" type fairing went on. This bike as used for extensive commuting and touring. When my brother-in-law took up production racing he raced it for several years with the only mechanical work needed the replacement of the rocker covers. Until he learned the right technique he used to grind them down very quickly on hard cornering. I´ve ridden it Adelaide to Bathurst ()1000K) and back on several occasions and found it comfortable and able to cruise at around 150K (90MPH) comfortably. The only drawbacks are the 4 speed box on the early models and the slight twitchiness at speed that the short wheelbase causes (compensated for by VERY quick cornering). He still has it some 30 years later.

author: NN
text: I´ve bought this bike in May 2001. It has 79000 miles on it. It hums along just great. I like the roarty sound of the twin. Reminds me of a Norton Commando sound. The new Beamers are pretty quiet with all the catalitic convertors and the like. The bike itself is in pretty good condition. It was owned by a gal. She said she just rode it around town. The bike, I was told sat for five years. When I first got it and was looking it over I found mice had made a nest in the air box.Access made available through the scoop at top of engine. Proud to own. Good part availability. Quality does last.

author: NN
text: 9-29-01, My 24 year old /7 is my daily rider. I can´t imagine it being much different now than when it was new. The clock is broken so I would guess it has 50-60k miles. mabye more, but I can ride 2-up,90mph @ 5000rpm´s all day in this Texas weather no problems. It´s not quick, but it´s not supposed to be. It does everything it is supposed to do and does it exceptionally well. handles everything I put it into, and takes everything I give it without a gripe. Sometimes it amazes me that it is such a wonderful bike.

Not bad for a port induction 250. If a Harley is a chopper, a Bul metralla is a razor.  The most precise handling motorcycle I ever rode. I finally sold it because I was going everywhere at illegal speeds. Great bike.

author: Paul
text: This is definately the frame number for Metralla Mk2.  The ´B´ represents ´Bastidor´ which is Spanish (Espanol) for frame. Then the first two digits of the serial number gives you the model number which is a ´model 23´.  If you were to look at a parts book for a Metralla Mk2, you would find that all the parts that are specific to this model will begin with 23.0----.  Hope this helps!

As for the oil plunger, that´s an easy item to reproduce providing you have one to copy from.  I never had one for mine so I can´t help you there even though I am a fabricator/welder/machinist. What surprises me is that some people are very reluctant to help. as for me I am missing the rear fender and can get no one to respond to even get the dimensions if I desire to make my own.  What I need is some one to send me a paper template of the fenders inner and outer circumferences.  The radius´ I can figure for myself.  Perhaps you can help me here. Paul

author: Brock Hansler
text: Hi Paul, Thanks for the info. I´ve emailed Don regarding the seat. On the oil pump plunger, I´ve seen one in a picture and it looks pretty straight forward. A billet disk with two heavy ´o´ rings stuck on a threaded shaft. If I don´t find one I´ll take a crack at making one. I´ve got the original housing so it´s a start. As far as the fender dimensions, I´ll be glad to help. If you will email me at captainkcorb@sympatico,ca I´ll send you a dimensioned drawing. I´ll be taking the bike apart for cleaning and painting so it won´t be much trouble. All the best, Brock

author: Adam
text: I went to a swap meet ten years ago to buy some kind of British crap. Didn´t see anything I liked. As I was leaving spotted this little ragged incomplete looking single with clip-ons. It looked almost complete, so I asked what was missing. The seller said "nothing, it´s all there and all original". Didn´t know anything about the bike or its history, but it started, and it looked good, so I bought it for $1400 AUS. Got the bike home, pulled it apart within 20 minutes, and then spent 2 years restoring it. Been riding it ever since. Goes great! Gets heaps of giggles from onlookers. But they soon stop giggling when they see it take off like a bullet. So goes my 1968 Bultaco Machine Gun. Had a Ducati 350 Desmo for a while, but it just wasn´t the same. The Duc´ went really well in a straight line, but felt like riding a sponge compared to the Metralla; so I sold the Duc´! Here´s some pointers on Metralla´s. The electrics are fine, really. The easiest thing to rectify the bulbs blowing is to simply fit a 12 volt bulb in the tail light. rev that little **** any which way, the bulb won´t blow! In fact for some strange reason fitting a 12 volt hallogen bulb to the headlight produces a brighter light! Don´t ask, it´s a Bultaco!. If you got the time, tear out the existing wiring and re-wire the entire bike with a heavier grade wire. 5 amp wire is better than the standard 3 amp. It´s a really simple task. As for things shaking to pieces. Tighten your bolts a little bit more. They´re obviously too loose. If anyone wants a really good Metralla contact me at artmn@hotmail.com Happy riding, Adam

author: Tony
text: Bought new in Canberra in 1970, Probably the best handling bike I´ve ever ridden in 30 years of riding. Quality control worse than any Italian bike ever built. It looked wonderful until everything fell to bits just out of warranty. Bits fell off, the paint flaked and the "electrics" (6 volt - horrible) died. Don´t let this deter you if you find a good one. The engine is good - lots of power for a 30 year old 250 single and basically reliable (if hard to start). You just need to check ALL the nuts & bolts daily - the vibration loosens them quickly although it is not too bad for the rider. Night riding is out - those lights! All in all though, there´s not another bike (apart from a good 450 Desmo) that I´d rather thrash through the twisty bits.

author: Anna Merikin
text: Stumbled across this discussion from Google, I bought a new ´68 in ´69 from the dealer in Milford, Connecticut, USA. I agree about the handling -- my first two-wheel drift! (And never one on any other bike!) That was one sweet, unforgettable ride. It would cruise all day at ninety and be happy as a post. The electrical system was out-of-date when it was built, but as it was a decent-enough source of sparks for a racing bike *which is what it was*, they just added primitive lights and a sort-of-muffler/expansion chamber for the street. A tip: keep a spare taillight bulb in the toolkit. The magneto uses the stoplight filament as ballast; this is a `feature´: an early brake light failure warning. ;o} Check the brake light bulb if you can´t figure out why it won´t start -- or suddenly stopped. I ran this bike hard and used nothing but Golden Spectro´s best 2-stroke oil at one ounce per gallon of gas. After 18,000 miles, out of boredom one winter, I took off the head just to inspect things, and the hone marks were still on the cylinder walls. This bike runs well on Golden Spectro! I´d love to obtain another, but the prices put me off. I paid US $675 for that Black and Silver sweetheart when new, and my only regret was selling it.

text: I had a Metralla and I found that the reason they were hard to start was that they had no choke. I would block the air intake and kick it over to get enough gas in the intake, then it would start fine. Messy, but effective.

author: P. Carter
text: Robbie, Didn´t you ever use the tickler on the carb float bowl?  Also you are not entirely correct in saying that there were no chokes.  The B389 Amal Monoblocks had them but when Bultaco switched to the Concentrics they were eliminated.  I had a 27mm Monoblock smoothbore originally on mine and dumped it in favor of the usual 30mm Mikuni.  That Monoblock would give me nothing but fits.  Often times the slide would stick open due to heat from the cylinder after only riding it for ten to fifteen miles.  It was rather embarrassing coming to a stop light and have the engine screaming away, leaving me no choice but shut it down, push to the curb, and let it cool down before continuing on.  Not fun! Paul

author: Colin Phillips
text: It sounds like one of your carburetor Diaphragms may have split. Pop the tops off (Mind the Spring!) and carefully remove the throttle bodies and pin assemblies & inspect the rubber. if there are any breaks or holes in them, that´s  your problem right there. Unfortunately, they are bonded to the throttle slides & can only be replaced with new units. It may also be likely that one of your contact breakers may have moved, or come loose inside the timing cover. My old girl does this from time to time. I tend to check the static timing regularly when the bike´s in regular use. Tip: If you find one of your diaphragms is damaged, make a hole in a small sandwich sized plastic bag and push it over the throttle slide (Needs to be a tight fit). Refit in the carb with the spring and clamp down with the fixing screws. Providing there are no other problems, your engine should now run smoothly. This bodge got me home one day when one of mine split, & I ran it like that for several days while the new one was on it´s way to me.

author: Ethan
text: Quick intro about me. I´m a 17 year old apprentice machinist and I have been restoring my 1982 CB250RSD on my own for about 7 months. I have managed to fix it up and it passed MOT and was on the road for about 2 weeks and then it was having some issues. It cut out on me twice while riding and then was a slight pain to start back up but I thought nothing of it to be quite honest. Then one morning I went to start it up for work and it just wouldn´t fire. Checked the spark plug which was fine as its pretty much brand new. Looked at the spark I was getting and it was a very thin blue spark but i´m not getting it every cycle. I have replaced the ignition coil and i´m now getting a stronger spark just still not on every cycle. Although I have restored the majority of the electrics on this bike, this one has got me stumped. The resistance from the pulser coil is within tolerance, so is the resistance for the ignition coil. Alternator and stator appear to be fine and I tried a second CDI unit and it gave the same outcome. Can anyone Help?

author: Petersenha
text: Hi. One of my daughters has bought what seems now, unmistakably, to be a Honda cb250n Super Dream 1979. After having ridden it home for 200km from where she bought it, it simply won´t start again. The spark plugs look pretty good. Holding them to the chassis produces a spark. Measuring with an ordinary digital multimeter between the cable and the sparkplug, shows 30 to 50 mA. Petrol in the cilinders: sparkplug moist with petrol, can smell petrol at the exhaust; tried, several times, injecting petrol into the bore with a syringe: not one fire. Tried jumpstarting with a car battery: one backfire. This was shortly after trying the fuel injection by hand. For about 30 seconds there were exhaust fumes, but I take it that was because of the manual fuel injections, because, trying the same jumpstart again very shortly after, there were no exhaust fumes, backfire or any sign of the motor wanting to fire up. Up to now we have had no sign whatever of the motor even thinking of starting up, as if it had no fuel or no spark. Our next objective is to figure out if there´s something wrong with the mixture, so: carburetor. My first guess is to fiddle around with the normally available settings for idle running and such. But how could a carburetor go so off after 200km´s riding? I´m not a very technically versed guy, but I like to do what I can and try to understand. Any help will be greatly appreciated.

author: PR Kershaw
text: I swapped an old scooter, for one from a mate of mine. He had no idea how to fix it, I didn´t know much about it. Fixed in 2 minutes a wire had come out. So I sorted out the engine, I was 17 at the time, so I smoothed out the ports, ground to valves, cleaned everything inside and out. Put some Clip ons on, rear sets, racing tyres, I don´t know if I did much to it I got around 125 out of it and the acceleration had come up wonderful. I altered the tank, and everything else in my money range which wasn´t much. I did however had to get another engine, the clutch push rod had a habit of knackering the oil seal. I did get one made out of EN8, my mate was a turner at the shipyard I worked at. That was after the event, but we live and learn. I cut my teeth on that bike, My brother left me in charge of a James 125 Villiers, while he was somewhere around the world in the Navy. I tried to get that going but I was younger still, he didn´t like it when I sold it for a tenner. I lost it to a house repossesson when I was on a year long bender after a divorce. I´d love another, but like rocking horse stuff. Very hard to find nowadays. Lovely bike,

These are a great little bike but they´re a town bike not a touring bike, if you keep it below 65mph the bikes are decent and great to learn on.

author: rahavens
text: dear regular guy: i have read tests,and stastics on the cb250. If you wish me to compare it to youre 450, here goes. While the 450 is strong enough for 2 up freeway riding, the 250 is not. You can ride it it solo on the freeway (is does not have power for the diamond lane). The 250 gets 100 miles per gallon. It can carry a passenger in town (as long as the tripp does not exceed 10 miles). The cb250 has the greatest safety record og all motorcycles in the united states) The cb250 is the finest economy bike on the market.

author: Jeff
text: I´m troubled by the Nighthawk´s low gear ratio. At 35mph I´m already shifting into 5th gear. For this reason I´ve never taken my bike onto the freeway. I´m considering either a smaller primary sprocket (1 to 2 teeth reduction) or a larger secondary sprocket. Has anyone else tried this approach?

author: richard havens
text: Jeff:There is a 30 tooth back sprocket which will gain you five mph crusing speed. what you also need is an electronic tachometer which is aviable for that bike. During break in do not exceed 4500 rpm during break in. afterwards do not exceed 5500 rpm. It would after break in be freeway safe if;you stay in the slow lane, do not try it with a passenger, and do not haul more than a tankbag full of baggage.

author: The Inspector
text: I increased the front sprocket from 14 to 15 teeth. Increased top speed and ease of cruising @ 65-70.  I have ridden on the highway over 100 miles at a time, and will be doing some 3 to 400 mile tours this summer. Great bike with great mileage. Added foam grips and a gel seat for comfort.

author: Ron
text: I bought the honda 250 Nighthawk and it is a great bike if you are riding solo.  I cant put the bike down for what it is but I am used to larger bikes.  Figure top speed to be between 70-75 mph depending on the highway grade.  Handlebar virations are aweful so a larger, smoother, lower reving engine would be more comfortable.  however it is sprinty and comfortable considering its size.  Mpg is super high.  I get 80.545 for that last two months use.  i want a goldwing but this gets me around.

Phil

author: Edison
text: I own this bike for over then three years and I am very satisfied with it.Lately I use super 98 octan(no lead) petrol and I sense more power from engine.During this time I didn´t have any problem with engine,except with some electrical parts(turn signalization relay,speedo instrument)which is probably due to long time use.Occassionally I do trips for about 500 km longs,and never left me down.I like it very much driving it and because of this bike now I´m hooked to Honda bikes.Honda rules!!! P.S. Honda cb 250 rs will allways be my nr.1 bike!

author: Crick
text: I ran a Honda 250 RS for about 4 years before I finally had enough of the continual stator problems. I have not noticed anyone else experiencing this problem, so my bike might have some other issues, perhaps a bent crank. But I want to say that I had a ball on this bike. Economical, low to the ground, reliable (apart from that one problem). It´s not good at anything, speed, handling, braking, but it is all good enough if you to ride as a commuter or tourist. I rode Melbourne to Queensland, 2800 kilometers for $80 of fuel. To me, that´s fun! Tyres are cheap and it was reliable...except for you know what. I now ride a TT 350 Yamaha dirt bike. Totally different experience. Rode a friends Honda 750. Totally different again. And now I´m considering putting the RS back on the road! Have to solve stator issues though.

Check out the CB250 Yahoo group.  They have lots of free speed mods for the hawk that will help you keep up with the larger bikes.

author: Steve
text: If you have the money to think about adding a ninja tranny you really should be looking for a new bike. I would just change the sprocket and maybe add some pods to it. Also just making sure you have correct tire pressure helps increase the top end. It´s not much obviously but it´s definitely worth it, keep the tire from uneven wear and prevents a blow out.

author: hutch
text: Just recently got my certification and I only have about 2 days exp. riding so far. Been looking at a few bikes and for the money this seems like a good starter bike. I would like to get a 600-750, but all the ones I have seen are a little out of my price range right now. My only question about this bike is, can it sustain speeds of about 65-70mph on the interstate without putting serious strain on the engine? Thanks for any feedback.

author: Paul
text: So long as you don´t go over the red line and keep the oil changed at least to schedule (I change mine more often) you can go as fast as you want for as long as the police and your wallet permit. Think of tire condition, condition of brakes, oil changes, condition of drive chain, etc.  That is, think preventive maintenance.  Don´t think of "strain," it simply isn´t an issue.  You can go 500,000 miles on that engine easy if you take care of things.  However, if you let your drive chain deteriorate to the point that it breaks and whips around and whacks a hole in your transmission or engine case, that´s as far as you go until you fix it. Paul

author: Steve
text: You don´t really have to worry about over reving the bike in 5th though. The air resistance is going to put enough strain on the bike that it will just stop accelerating and not over rev. If you´ve only got 2 days riding exp. Don´t get a 600. I work at a dealer/repair shop and we refer to riders who buy bigger bikes as \

author: Chris
text: Please Help Someone...., my 75 cb250 has developed a problem with the cam chain tensioner which has left me, my brother and my old man confused....PLEASE HELP ANYONE...we´re in the process of getting this thing ready for racing(!! yes it´s true) and have discovered that when the rear tensioner is fitted into it´s top and bottom ´cups´ and tensioned the chain is still quite loose.We don´t think the chain has stretched that much nor the tensioner itself. It´s almost as if the tensioner needs to be another 10mm longer. When the tensioner spring is released thus ´squeezing´ the tensioner and pushing it against the chain, the bottom cup comes up and there´s a tang on the cup which registers with the casting preventing it from coming up any higher...is this right?

author: Yo bro wassap
text: The cheapest and easiest way is to change the size of the gear wheel front (make larger) or rear (make smaller) .  Although you can increase top speed, your power output on the rear wheel will be adversely affected. Also, a higher top speed also means increased drag. The only thing that can overcome increased drag in a fixed design is an increase in power.(Which you don´t have.) Try a 400 cc engine:-)

author: Yo bro wassap
text: Did I mention that changing the chain gears (to increase top speed) also puts higher stress on bearings and transmission?

author: Phill
text: This comment on the Honda CB250RS is quite true. I have one myself that I bought while I was saving up to get a 1000cc rocket machine. I still have the 250 because it blows my mind with its overall performance. I honestly doubt that there are any road bikes as reliable and as durable as the Honda CB250RS. It can do 160kph, wheelstand up the road 1st and 2nd gear, not bad for little guy with one pot and 24 years behind it, plus I also found it to be very excellent off road as well, taking jumps, slides, and power drift in gravell and dirt, best fun I have had on any motorcycle far better than any Harley that has ever been made.

author: NN
text: I bought this bike in late 2000, and have had it since (2002). I live in Holland, and have been to Belgium, England, France, Luxembourg and Germany with it in the summer of 2001. The bike is really reliable, although there are some minor problems. The most anoying was the braking problemn, escpecially if the original hydrolic rubber tube is still fitted, you get slugish braking, and you can pull the brake lever all the way back, untill it bumps your steeringgrip. I solved the problem by fitting a stainless-steel tube (40 Euro), and now it brakes like never before. An other big problem is on the rear wheel. There is a ditch for fixing the rear chain-gear with a circlip, which is almost always worn out on all bikes. That results in a lot of clearance on the rear chain-gear, and eventually braking loose. The only option is a new rearwheel. But on overall, the bike is great fun to ride, but don´t count on big performance, especially not on the highway. The maximum speed without frontwind is about 110 kmh. If you have winds, then sometimes you don´t reach above 85-90 kmh.

author: Alan Lamont
text: Bought secondhand fro £350. It was fast, but no good on corners, prone to tank slapping unless the damper was made tight.  Fuel cons around 35-40 mpg no matter how it was driven.  Basically a cheap robust large heavy Japanese 2-stroke. Not too bad on tyres, a bit hard on chain and sprockets. It was rebored at about 35000 miles.  Chrome parts did surprisingly well.  It eventually wore out its kickstart ratchet and finally died after it cracked a cylinder head close to a bolt hole, which is common at high mileage no secondhand ones were around.  Eventually repaired it after tracking down specialist parts supplier, not a difficult DIY rebuild, and sold it on for peanuts.

author: TwoStrokesRule
text: - Your note: hello biker people.. I have just got my hands on an old 350 yamaha. now the serial number on the motor starts with R5-______.  - now,that,i believe means the motor was made in 1970. The gas tank color is tangerine and white , which i think is the colors for 1971. So am i correct in assuming that the motor was built in 1970 but out on a frame and sold as a 1971? and am i correct in that the motors were excectly the same in 70,71,and 72? thanks charlie.  REPLY: Charlie  - I have two (2) Yamaha R5 350cc two stroke twins; (1) is VIN: R5-00331 (1970) and (1) is VIN: R5-121195 (1972).  Engine and frame for both have the same VIN.  - Internals are the same but there are some minor differences in external cases.  I have riden the 1970 for the past 10+ years.  Since it is piston port and the later RD350 is reed valve induction the pistons are different and not made by aftermaket (ie. Weisco), so all that is available is NOS Yamaha; and, they are expensive!  Top end can be converted to RD (Cyls, Pistons, Reed Vlv., Carbs, Heads); but, you will still only have a 5 speed gear box.  This is why most people prefer the RD.  I am presently in the process of putting an RD250 engine into my 1970 R5 and working on two other engines I have (an RD350 and an RD400).  I will keep the R5 engine in case I find reasonably prices pistons somewhere; but, for now, I´m looking for something to ride.  ... Dick J - NY

author: Scott
text: This was the second motorcycle I owned. I got it in 1972 or 1973. It was extremely fast, good handling and reliable. The only real problems I ever had with mine were fouling plugs, which I later found out better ignition coils would have solved. It also went through a couple of voltage regulators, a problem I had with several of the yamahas I had from the seventies. You never did see very many 1970 R5´s, even back then. Every time I do see one it brings a smile and a trip down memory lane.

author: John Hannaford
text: Bought mine in April 1973 from Commerfords in London.  Wheelies all the way home.  After my YAS1 125 this was a bullit machine.  Nearly wrote it off 4 weeks later when a car turned right as I was overtaking it.  Repaired under insurance.  Started production racing with it at the end of 1974 and got lapped in my first race - damn.  That was when I realised it was harder than it looked!  Started the 1975 season determined never to be lapped again and got my first win later that year.  1976 I went to the Isle of Man with it for the Manx Grand Prix - man what a rush.  Clocked at 120mph on the fast bits.  DNF in the race due to front tyre blow out at 120 !! Still got the yam, and after I retired it from proddy racing in 1978, I put the road bits back on and used it to commute to work for a short while.  It is now moth-balled in the garage. My verdict - a fantastic machine, and only the Kawasaki 500H triple beat me in proddy racing.  Awesome machine for £363.00.

author: Speed
text: Whoa, you must be running way too rich!  I think I got about 50 to 60 mpg or so with mine back in the late 70´s.  My best run was against a 250 motocross bike on a logging trail in the woods.  I kept having to slow down for the mud puddles on the curves (those street tires got no bite in the mud!).  He challenged me to a drag when we got to the highway, him on the shoulder me on the blacktop, and I left him in the dust right from the starting line.  Mine would top out at about 91 mph no matter how tight I hugged the tank.  But, then again, it would be wobbling so badly by then that I wouldn´t want to go faster.  It took work to keep the front tire down on a fast start.  You should be getting this kind of performance.  As far as I can tell, it had an engine similar to that of the present day Banshee in a very light street bike (hmmm, maybe you could find a Banshee engine and do a little swap).

(Since that´s the one I own)

author: NN
text: You are correct about the R5 series. I owned a 1972. It was my first true street bike. I have lots of great memories from the days riding that machine. It liked to foul plugs until I put automotive coils in it. If I left the gas tap on it would fill the crankcase with gas. I didn´t forget too often! That bike could really handle.

author: colin.easson
text: Bought one brand new in 1972. Kickstart broke in the first month as it was a pig to start. Used it for 150 miles a day round trips between Wolverhampton & Sheffield, exhaust fell off 7 times one night coming back to Wolverhampton. Rubber muffler in exhaust perished and could not get a replacement so bike waas embrassing to ride due to the noise. Electrics where hopeless, especially in wet weather. Front forks where like rubber and could be adjusted by gripping the forks between you knees and twisting the handle bars. Ride was very good for its size and the exhuast kept one warm in winter. Luggage rack was small but carried my bag of clothing and tools. Engine was a strong puller and maintained 60 mph no bother, can´t remember fuel consumption figures. Brakes where drums and proved adquate. Moved onto a Honda CB175K6 after the night of the falling exhaust pipe.

author: Giulio Mazzone
text: Benelli 125 sport was a great bike built in the late 70´s - early 80´s. It was very powerful for that time,its engine power was about 17 hp, with a max speed of 135 km\h. The engine sound was similar to competition bike one, this was due to two cilinders of the engine. The look was very aggressive, at least in those years. Its main negative aspect was the smoke from the exausts, that made unpractical its usage for the drivers, who felt oil anywhere on their clots. Apart from that, it was a really great bike. Nowdays it is very rare to find it in Italy, I think that nobody will see it anymore. Tough. Giulio

author: Rob
text: I had a P-reg orange and black job of this bike between 1984-85. As far as 125´s went it was one of the physically biggest, much bigger than the jap 125´s because it shared the same chassis as the 250. It was a terrific bike, the 69mph quoted seems all wrong, I saw 85mph on the instruments (borne out by others along side me). I loved my Benelli, it had real presence and individuality. Despite being big it was (typical for Italian machinery) flat, the seat and the tank were level so it felt sporty. It was quick, being a twin rather than the usual single for a bike of this size, 180 degrees so that when my retarded friend swapped the plug caps over it still worked! The handleing was everything you´d expect from an italian bike, solid, sure and importantly, forgiving, one of the best I have had in this area. Drawbacks? Well it was a premix 2-stroke, so either you found a good garage or you carried oil with you. I once pushed it 4 miles to get to a garage that could do it (I was poor!). On mine the gearbox was ropey, I got it fixed but it went again so I lost 1st gear. Not a big drawback, but you´d rather have it wouldn´t you? Overall, a handsome and solid bike, beautiful alloy cast engine, that handled wonderfully and didn´t feel or look like a 125. If you can find one buy it, keep hold of it and love it, I wish I had.

author: François Ph. Thomas
text: It´s a great bike, the last production of 2 valves boxer engines ; only 3003 units were built in Spandau (Berlin) in 1996. It was never imported in Belgium, only 50 in France, and I´ve been told only 15 in the UK. There are lots of components to tune the engine and transmission, supensions and brakes, although it´s a very nice bike to ride in it´s original condition, and it´s quite easy to find spare parts. I sold my R 1150 GS Adventure (the best bike I´ve ever had) to ride the R 80 GS Basic because I like it´s charm more than performance and efficiency. But I also enjoy riding the R 80 G/S, the one with monolever and 18´ rear wheel, which is much lighter, but the front fork needs to be changed for a good one such as the Marzocchi that equipped the paralever models.

author: Sami
text: I have a R 80 Gs Basic and will start riding in May 06. I was thinking sending my bike to HPN or buying some parts and do it ourselves? Thanks a lot for comments. Sami

author: B. Schönfelder
text: I ride a new bought R80 GS Basic since 1998 until now 49000 KM. At 45000 KM I needed a new sealing of the rear paralever gearbox (wheel drive) an at 47000 KM a new generator. The generator suffers from high temperatures (above 25 Centigrade air temperature an above 5000 rpm it will break down earlier). The white power rear suspension increases the possible curve speed remarkable compared with other two valve boxer bikes. The last few kilometers the ignition had temporarily failure above 25 Centigrade air temperature.

author: guy fasanaro
text: The 1987 Elefant has been,(until the KTM 950 V-twin) the most competent Adventure/rally type motorcycle on the market.While at 50hp,it is not as fast as a bmw gs,it is 140lbs lighter and has great torque from the ducati pantah L-twin motor. the 42mm Marzocchi M1 forks,Brembo brakes and Ohlins shock are great stuff,and the build quality refects the Castiglione brothers´passion for well engineered motorcycles.The Elefant handles amazingly rough terrain with ease and confidence, and is rock-steady and vibraton-free on the highway.This bike was way ahead of it´s time.

author: Aaron Khoo
text: Hello Charlie DelForge,  I´m Aaron Khoo from Singapore - I just bought a dr200 2007 model.  The retailer did not inform me that it was a 2007 model until I decided to buy the bike after seeing it for a few times at the retail bike shop in singapore.  The lesson I learnt is that in future we as bikers must be bold to QUESTION the seller in order to clear any doubts we have - that is called customer rights, after all, we are the buyers.  The seller should give you a DR200 manual - a manual is not an option - it should be given to you as the price of the bike INCLUDES the manual - same for a television or a refrigerator.  Be bold, start asking the seller for the manual - otherwise how else will you be able to know how to MAINTAIN your bike!

author: nobby\'s
text: can some please tell me how to get the ratio for the stock gears i add a 90/90/21 front and a 120/100/18 rear now the bikes tops speed is aprox. 35 kph slower i would like to add more teeth to the rear sprocket but how many to correct the loss is what im after

author: Brad
text: If you lost top end speed you will need a smaller back sprocket not a larger one. Or better yet, install a larger front sprocket.  I have the same bike and I´m trying to get a larger front sprocket to make highway driving a bit better. I want to lower my rpm at highway speeds.  I have checked with all the after-market suppliers and none of them list optional sprocket sizes for your bike.  Presenty you have a 15 tooth front sprocket and a 45 tooth rear sprocket. A 3 to 1 ratio. Front sprocket turns 3 times, back sproket (wheel) turns once. As you can see, if you had a 30 tooth rear sprocket then you would have a 2 to 1 ratio. Front sprocket only has to turn twice now. So now if the front sprocket did turn  3 times (representing same engine rpm) the back wheel will now turn 1 and a half turns. This would be a HUGE increase in speed. In fact the bike would be poor off road because first gear would be like second! What you want is maybe a 16 tooth front sprocket or maybe a 42 back sprocket. You will probably have to bring the bike into a shop so they can see the type of sprockets to know what fits. I´m sure different ones will fit, they just don´t list them in the books because people don´t usually change sizes on a bike like that. Be aware that the front sprocket is inside a casing and if you go too big the chain could rub. And if your back sprocket is too small you might not be able to adjust the chain tension properly. Knowing the gear ratios in the transmision is of no help because you can´t change anything there. Be aware that it doesn´t take much of a size change to get noticable results. You can also take your old back tire, put a mark on it and roll it exactly one turn on the ground and then measure the distance.  Then do the same for your new back tire. The new smaller tire will have a shorter distance. The difference in the two measurements is all you have to make up.  If your good at math you can figure out what sproket sizes will solve the problem. Good luck.

author: Dave 1985 K100RS
text: I also just bought a 1985 K100RS last month.  (Had a R75/6 back in 1977 until 1999 and finnally bought another bike.) Had to do work on the instruments to repair the clock and gear displays and get all the indicator lights working.  Got tool and new metal crimp hose clamps to reseal manifold and throttle body hoses to eliminate air leaks.  Will have to replace the fork seals soon.  Changed oil, filter, fuel filter, trans. oil, rear drive oil, shaft oil.  Still have to flush coolant.  Found a set of BMW guages for water temp and a voltmeter with the brackets to go in the RS fairing - ordered cables - will install once cables come in. Glad to exchange resources I´ve identified and anything I learn myself. Dave

author: Clarke
text: Bike has been running good but a few days ago I noticed the tach was internment, then turn signals and horn quit working.  Bike doesn’t turn over now, but if you keep hitting starter switch it will eventually turn over.  Headlight and taillight works, and does not dim while pushing starter button. Battery was replaced a few months ago and I verified it’s still good.  Cables were replaced a few years ago.  I cleaned all the ground connections on the frame.  Also cleaned all positive connections including both connections on starter solenoid. Replaced starter relay and removed/cleaned solenoid plunger, and still have same results.  I also connected jumper wire from battery to solenoid and get the same results (does not turn over but eventually will turn over). I noticed the starter circuit breaker/fuse that keeps clicking also gets warm to touch while the key is on. I don’t know how to troubleshoot from here?  I have a volt meter but not an exp expert on circuity. Thanks in advance.

author: Mad Man !
text: I´m an Old Biker and a retired mechanic who´s been riding a long time now ! . I decided This winter to get an Antique bike and to restore it as a Custom but to stay kind of close to The Original model with my own rat bike touches like ; Tuned Drag Pipes, Zenith Panhead Carb , a single seat, fork gators, black spokes and My Own HD Decals ! I did all that and it looks great, I wanted this for a weekend bike to take to bike shows but I like to actually ride my bikes also and not to just look at them through a beer glass. So now I have this 76 XLH 1000 with 5,500 miles and everything is working good on the bike except that the engine shakes like hell at 40 to 50 mph and it has some vibration tremors at idles , vibrations that actually rattle and shake the front wheel and the forks ! , I can understand now why it was parked for 23 years before I got it ! . I like the bikes ride and performance in general and it´s fast enough for what I want it for and I wondered why no one seems to say much of anything about  Iron head 1000 Crank Balancing Problems with their engines ? A Twin Cylinder Shaking Is something I can handle , I rode a 68 BSA 650 Twin, A Lightning hard tail when I was Young and I had a Hard Tail 73 750 TX Twin also so I am no stranger to engine vibrations , but what this Harley is doing is totally sick and is boarder line on psychotic ! , If I bought a new bike that shook that bad I would return it instantly and not take it back from the dealer until it was fixed ! . This bike at 40 to 50 mph feels like it´s gonna snap the front forks and goose neck off the bike , it almost bounces the front wheel!. I know I need to tear it down to see if someone has been in there before and messed with the pistons and crankshaft before I blame HD for making such an earth shaker!. I guess what I am wondering about is did some of these bike shake like that when they were new ?!.With the problems the new ones are having with cranks slipping and timing chains I have to ask ? I have a Honda Shadow also and it shakes some but it´s nothing like this bike.

text: I would be grateful for people´s help confirming some details about the 1972 Harley-Davidson FLCH1000 Sportster. I am putting the finishing touches to a book I’ve written about superbikes of the 1970s. My questions are: 1. How much did the XLCH Sportster retail for in the UK in 1972 (I know it cost $2,025 in the US)? 2. What were the standard colour schemes for the Sportster in 1972? 3. What was the ’72 XLCH’s top speed and average fuel consumption? Finally, grateful to anyone who would consider letting me use pictures of a completely stock ’72 Sportster for use in my book (pictures that don´t infringe anybody´s copyright!). I would be happy to include full picture credits. Kind regards, Dave Sheehan | Sheffield | South Yorkshire | ENGLAND | david.sheehan@dwp.gsi.gov.uk | davesheehan21@hotmail.co.uk |

author: smckee
text: Hello, It depends if it needs a little or a lot of work. I am told that one in decent shape is in that ballpark, and that a fully showroom restored and rebuilt 72 could be roughly valued at up to 7200.00.  Google Sportsters of that vintage for sale and you will find a range of prices, and descriptions of the general condition of each bike.  Write down half a dozen, take a few notes on each and the asking price, and you will be able to compare the one he is looking at. Scott

author: Cliff Rieth
text: Starting quite a project. It started with a recently purchased 1972 XLCH Sportster that after the first 100 miles shut down at normal driving speed. I quickly realized this bike was neglected a bit before me. Turned out the rod bearing about seized on me. I since removed the engine piece by piece. Sent the flywheel and jugs to a machinist and got new rods, rod bearings, pistons/ rings, fresh bore on heads and a completely new valve job. Also kept going....clutch plates, all new motor bolts, stainless hoses, powder coating the frame and swing arm....THE WORKS. I was going to go for the electric start but the kick start is the PERSONALITY of the bike. It should look sweet when all is done.

text: I´m new to owning a harley sportster, and the 1975 that I just bought has a ss super carborator.  Wondering how to adjust as seems to be running  to rich, can anyone help me out with this problem?

author: ironhead75
text: s&s is very easy. best to adjust during riding season. warm it up. hold at 2000 rpm and adjust idle mixture in until it breaks up then out until it breaks up, then split the difference. now crank the throttle if it boggs, back off the accellerator pump til it stops bogging. ride it then check the plugs, if brownish. okay. if black or fouled try removing the main jet and reducing size a little at a time. don,t make it too lean! cheaper than a dyno!

text: Hi Scott, I ride and wrench on a 1973 Ironhead Sportster. The 1973 year had a mid year change in the cylinders and heads, an \

author: Frigeratorman
text: You are correct, there was no neutral switch for the 1973 model year with no indicator light of such. Therefore, the 2 holes for indicator lights are for oil pressure and generator. author: Mike Stone
text: The Sportster has a clutch problem.  First it started sticking and wouldn´t disengage until the engine warmed up.  Now it both sticks and once it warms up it slips in 3rd and 4th gears.  Also the clutch chatters when cold and sounds like gears are slipping.  I have used info. in the manual to adjust the clutch, but that didn´t help.  Any ideas why it both grabs and slips?  Thanks

author: Nick Mallow
text: I would have to say to replace your clutch plates first. I somewhat adjust my clutch to the book. The book says to screw the adjustment screw in two turns. Then take all of the slack out of the cable with the barel screw, then back off the scerw so you have an 1/8 inch of play in your lever. What I do is turn the screw in two turns, then take all of the slack out of the cable. Back off the screw untill it is compleatly free of the outer plate, then back in untill it hits the outer plate. Finaly adjust your barel screw so you have that 1/8 inch of play in your lever. You should also check your oil level in the case. If it is low or high it could so that too. put a 2x4 under the kickstand to bring the bike more level. pull out the plug out that is in the case that is by the foot peg. pull out the fill plug and add the oil untill it starts to come out of the plug that is by the foot peg. that is it correct amount of oil that should be in the case.

author: mn
text: I have a 72 sportster with a Crane Hi4 electronic ignition, a new solid-state regulator and S&S shorty E carb.  The bike ran great for 1000 miles after installing the new parts but then started to load up a bit (bogging, then backfiring thru the exhaust).  Opening the throttle would blow it out, but the more I rode it (the hotter it got) the worse it ran until I wasn’t sure I could get home.  At a steady 2-3K rpm it  totally misfires and backfires thru the exhaust.  The only way I could get home was to downshift (to up the rpm’s) and floor it.  Otherwise, at low rpm it would just pop, spit and backfire.  It would still idle when I let off the gas, but roughly, and the rpm’s were very erratic.  After letting it sit me for 15 minutes, it started right up and idled ok.  I didnt take it on the road, but suspect that it wouldnt have run well until it was totally cooled down.  This obviously sounds like a heat related issue, but I’m not sure what component is being affected.  Seems like spark, but I suppose it could also be fuel.  I have the thermal isolator between the intake manifold and the carb like the carb manufacturer recommended and didn’t have any problems with the carb before all of this started.  Has anyone ever heard of coils or electronic ignitions doing this from heat?  Suggestions or previous experience is appreciated.

author: Sportster
text: Check your push rods. If your hearing alot of noise they are too loose, hard to start maybe too tight. Set them where they spin in your fingers with hardly any side shake.  Get it started, warm it up,and re-check. If they are too tight your valves will not close properly. I,m kind of new at this myself. I´ve been restoring my 73 XLCH and have been getting great advise from sites like this one. You might want to go to the S&S Super E. Its more suited for Sportsters. I´ve stayed with the original Bendix Zenith. I´m determined to keep my bike original. O I´ve re-chromed everything, powdered coated the frame, oil tank, and chain guard,polished the forks and front hub. I´ve had the wheels re-laced and the fenders and tank re-painted. Engine was re-built. I did change the exhaust though. I thought I would jazz it up with drag pipes. Got it started and it sounded great. I was in the process of timing it and had kick start problems. I´m waiting on a new ratchet gear. The bushing on the original was worn out and not allowing the ratchet gear to engage the way it should. Sorry about ramblin on but I´m finding out that even I can do most everything on the bike. Get a manual and you´ll save around $70.00 and hour. Good luck.

author: scottfultz
text: i was wondering, could any one tell me or give me a tip on how to spot the carb burning to much gas! rather the needed amount I have a fear on this ss carb and the shaking of a 74 Iron head i know the jets will adjusting from time to time so is there any one out there can give me a tip on spotting quickly the carb burning to much gas

author: Sully
text: too much fuel can be determined by smell (the fumes will burn your eyes), by sight ( black smoke out of the exhaust) and fouled plugs. The only jets that should need adjusting is the idle mixture as the main metering jet is a fixed orifice that you change out to find the sweet spot. If it runs, rides and is there when you need it, I wouldnt worry too much till you get a better grasp of how it all works together. That engine was designed for leaded gas so running rich will save your valve seats.

text: I have the same exact bike and I have to say that the overall best  book is  Clymers books, you can order them online just as fast and cheaper than oredering it at your local shop. They have been very good for all my overall needs whenit comes to this particular bike, and have some very good still shots to guide you along, other than that the next best thing would be an actual Harley shop manual, but not only are they hard to get but if your not familiar with all the parts  as described than it may not be as helpful as the Clymers book, simply because they are made for actual mechanics who have the experiance already( i say this not know how much experiance you actually have)As far as aftermarket parts, hey that depends, I have had great luck with various aftermarket catalogs that i keep a stock of, such as Custom Chrome, JP´s and for some more hard to find parts Foghollow, but i ahave also been able to find some parts on Ebay, the only thing is you have to know for sure what you are looking for and ask the proper questions to make sure before placing the orders otherwise your just disappointed when they show and dont fit. author: Little Joe
text: I read  alot of the questions and answers but it didnt seem to help so  its my turn to ask....My sportster leaks all the time, sitting still or running, I always see the oil (mind you not big time) just that i can see it on my floor when it sits, and on my frame after i have been riding it. first question is will changing to a heavier wieght oil (50 straight)rather than the standard 20/50 help this, and the second question is is there anything else other than leaking past the valves or changing the gaskets that can help (I have replaced the gaskets and replaced the valve guides) Thanks, Little Joe

author: cyclshay
text: Little Joe, I don´t know how much help I can be for you, but, I am currently rebuilding a 74 sportster. I am not an expert mechanic, but I am definitly learning alot as I go. I noticed that you didn´t specify if the leak was top engine or bottom. There are a TON of gaskets and seals on this bike, not just between the crank and the heads.I currently have my entire casing in half and I am going through every inch of this motor. You could have a leak between gearcase and its housing, or it could be a bad checkvalve in the oil pump. I have been told that this is a commom problem with these old bikes. There is a oil pressure switch on the right side of the oil pump,beyond that is a oil pump nipple, then there is a check valve spring with a valve ball. If this spring is weak or ball worn, it will allow oil to seep past and make a big mess. I hope some of this info can be helpful to you because something like this can drive you nuts! Good luck.

author: Sully
text: You should be running the 74 Ironhead on straight 50 weight oil. I wager your leaks are from the 20W50. I had the same issue and it runs cooler with the straight weight as well.

Please tell me how to check and see if mine has one.  Mine has a right side kick start and a electric ignition on the left side.  Thanks.

author: mikethebike
text: a 1972 should not have a mag. If it does you will have a cable from the left handle bar grip that goes through the bar to the mag for advancing/retarding the ignition. Plus, the plug wires will go directly to the mag instead of to a coil...which mags don´t use.

The Evo engine is more practical , but heh don´t this and certainly the Twin Cam seem too sanitised - dare I say it BLAND - most horror of horrors like a Japenese Bike - Characterless ...but reliable ect. No if you want somthing that would not be allowed today -SMOKE LOUD RUMBLING slice of HEAVY METAL THUNDER - accept no substitute.. but buy one that is being ridden ! Break out from the rabble !!!

author: Casey
text: I bought a ´74 XLCH in 1974 and still have it.  Needs a little TLC but runs strong.  I´m new to this site so forgive me if this is old news... In 1974 we were still running ´leaded´ fuel.  When I had the heads ported/polished in the 80´s the valve seals had to be upgraded to accept the new ´un-leaded´ fuels. I also recall a jetting adjustment on the old S&S to run consistently through the power band with open pipes. Glad I found this site and I´ll be passing thru again soon.

author: mrbones
text: Hello Brian!

author: mrbones
text: One thing that you might check before doing anything like buying a new carb or whatever is that you might have an intake manifold leak. That will cause the motor to pop. The factory seals on the intake are not a very good design. Go find you an tube out of a 20" bike tire and cut 2 pieces about an inch wide. Install these pieces over the stock gaskets where the intake joins the heads. (it is a procedure that will probably make you cuss too!) Wrap them up with a little electrical tape and then put the clamps back on. Let me know if this solves your problem.

author: SH
text: I found out the hardway, you should not have to.. To see if a bike is listed on the insurance ´stolen´ data base (they NEVER purge these records, and if yours shows up on it when you go to get it titled, you will lose your bike, the engine, the engined cases, or perhaps all of the above. HD has numbers on the bottoms of the cases (under the bike) you can only see with a mirror or when the engine is out and laid over on its side.  These numbers are recorded by HD when built and they link to the engine number (VIN) on the case, that you see at the top of the case.  It has stars on both sides, on the older bikes. I found out that my engine was stolen 28 years ago, and had been registered under a modified VIN (they changed the 6´s to 8´s on the number.  Your local hiway patrol will run the numbers on the cases (the bottom) and see if it matches up with the Engine number. If it does not, you got yourself a stolen engine or at least the VIN has been changed and they must (by law) confiscate the engine, or possibly the whole bike. -so be careful.  I am trying to get the guy I bought from to help pay for new cases and labor.  A real ****!!!  hope this helps and prevents you from going through the crap I am now.

author: Michael L Lynch
text: My regulater has 3 wires coming from it,red,green,and a tan one.Do the green and tan go to the generater,and the red to the swith? also what would be the point gap,and the plug gap? If the generater is hooked up wrong,would it cause the bike to miss? It fires up but misses like crazy

author: Pos
text: red goes from the (B+) on the regulator to the switch & the + side of the battery green goes from the (DF) on the regulator to the field on the generator (F) tan goes from the (D+) on the regulator to the armature on the generator (A)and to the Gen. light indicator. Point gap should be 0.020 Plug gap should br 0.025-0.030 The only reason for the gen to cause it to miss is if you were running off of the battery and the charge ran low so you were not getting enough juice to the points. If it starts and runs smooth eith a full charge on the bat, then yess the gen not charging could be a problem. Check the points and the timing Timing advanced 40 deg btdc Timing retarded 10 deg btdc Send me your e-mail address and I´ll send you a wiring diagram

author: James Murphy
text: I have an 80 sportster and it has a transmission leak. Several attemps from various dealers to fix the leak have failed. Any help out there?

author: Richard Rivera
text: This answer may be a few months too old ;first check that your crankase venting system is working and your trany is not "making oil", the shops should have changed the seals and gaskets at the potential leaks; primary cover gasket, drain bolt seal, crank seal, shifter shaft seals, sprocket seals, siliconing the exhaust mounting stud/bolt, the filler plug o-ring and clutch adjusting plug o-ring, if any of those were the problem. If the problem persists I sugest getting the manual out and checking and/or replacing these items, all quite simple and can be done with common hand tools

author: ensor7318
text: I just bought a 74 XLCH, this summer. It is a strong running bike, but I have a problem with the starter hanging up. I have to disconnect the battery to get it to disengage. It only seems to do this if the battery charge is low. I have replaced the solenoid and the generator (the old 1 was worn out). Is there something I can do to eliminate this problem or should I just learn to live with it?

author: Sully
text: sounds like you might not have a relay installed between your start switch and the starter solenoid. Especially if it is a new or aftermarket solenoid the coil inside of it requires a good amount of current to push the gear into engagement with the clutch drum.My bike was a kick-only till I scrounged up the parts to get it right. you didnt mention if it stays engaged after it starts which would be the drive gear. Did you replace the spring under the solenoid? The relay I am talking about goes inline from the battery to the small terminal on the solenoid and it is controlled by the start switch wherever you have it(mine is on the frame behind my left leg cause I smoked the tiny one in the handlebar controls) Mine is a 74 also which I have done everything but split the cases on so lets compare notes. FYI the alternator conversion kit from J&P cycles didnt work out.

author: Rick ensor7318
text: I put a relay in the positive battery cable and it eliminated that problem. The starter motor would be running but not engaged to the flywheel.

author: Sully
text: That is odd but a good hack. I havent had that particular problem and since my battery is buried inside my horseshoe shaped oil tank I think it would be quite the **** to disconnect in a hurry.

author: check this
text: unhook starter and check for voltage shouldnt be any till the switch is pushed switch may be broke

author: Jason
text: Hey guys i just recently purchased a 1972 Sportster 1000XLH.It is right hand shift and is all original.I´m the third owner.It has 8,300 original miles and looks brand new(Garage Kept).I was wondering how much gear oil it´s supposed to hold,and how to measure it,and do i fill it on the left side of the engine through the screw out cap.Thanx for any info.Jason

author: Greg Robertson
text: I also own a ´72 XLH, although yours sounds like it is in a lot better shape. I would suggest that you obtain either the Clymer HD Sportsters 1959-1985 service manual or the Haynes HD Sportsters 1970-2001 "Owner´s Workshop Manual." Both of these books are available through the internet at a discount price (simply run a search for the titles or model). Be careful with the bike! The drum brakes are not the same as the newer disk brakes, and the shifter being on the right side instead of the left can get you into real trouble even if it is not an emergency. When I first rode mine, I almost ran into a car because I kept pushing the shifter instead of the rear brake. Take care, Greg

author: firefighterdan
text: Crank out the big flat head fill screw. Found that a wonder bar works awesome. Just the right size. And below, about 6 inches and a little to the left there will be a hex bolt. Crank that lil baby out also. Make sure the bike is level and not on the kick stand. Add fluid till it runs from the hex bolt hole. Use the same oil in your tranny as in your engine. Rule of thumb is never to mix different brands of oil. If in doubt drain ur Hog and fill er´ up fresh. I was lucky enough to still have the original owners manual.

author: ensor7318
text: I have a 74 XLCH I just bought. The previous owner was hice enough to give me a service manuel for it. The engine oil capacity is 3 quarts (Mine has a later model oil tank and exsternal oil filter and cooler so it is more like 4 qts.) The tranny capacity is 1.5 Pints. Most people recomend 20w50 for the engine and tranny. I just had the clutch replaced at my local shop and they filled the tranny with an 80 weight synthedic oil. I run Mobil 1 (15W50) in my engine and am thinking of putting it in the tranny next oil change.

author: Sully
text: Straight weight 50 for both, the quantities are right but mine runs cooler and doesnt smoke at all with the 50wt oil. The trans shifts a whole lot better too with it than the 20w50 I had been using. For fuel I have been running a lot of 100LL avgas from the airport I work at which while the LL stands for Low Lead, it has 4 times the lead they used to use in car gas. It is a little pricey but it runs so smooth, smells sweet, is bright blue in color, and it turns the inside of my drag pipes white!

author: Rick
text: I had a new clutch put in my sportster a few weeks ago at my local bikeshop (It is a Titan dealership) and i cant seem to get the clutch adjusted properly. If you adjust it so that it grabs as soon as you put it in gear, it is rideable but if you adjust it so that it isn´t trying to pull with the clutch disengaged, it slips like hell. I think I need to change the spacers for shorter ones but I´m not sure. I wish now that I had just installed it myself but my work didn´t leave me the time.

author: Sully
text: you might just need to wear it in a little bit due to the fact that you have new plates and spacers that will "take a set" after a little burn-in. worst case try taking one set of plates out or make sure they didnt put too many in. be aware though that you will want to make or buy a spring compressor cause otherwise it is a hell of a job getting the pressure plate back on one of the studs far enough to start one of the self locking nuts ( I do it by hand but not for fun). Try a different oil ( you will want to change it anyway since it is shared with the transmission and you dont want the clutch break-in crud fouling it up.

author: Ensor7318
text: I have a clutch spring compression tool. I think I will change the tranny oil back to 50 weight. they had put a heavy weight synthetic oil in it (90 weight I think).

author: Greg Robertson
text: I´m just buying one myself (1972 XLH). It´s in good enough shape to restore; almost everything is original and it´s been in storage for about 20 years. Shifting is one-down and three-up which will take you through all four gears. The bike has the foot brake on the left and the shifter on the right, which can be confusing and dangerous in an emergency. They have laws these days about that, but in 1972 they didn´t. Good luck with the bike! Greg

author: howard
text: Panhead looks like a brownie pan. Knucklehead has very large nuts on the rocker shafts resembling knuckles. There are no rocker boxes on a flathead, it is a side valve construction. Blockhead is squared looking. (blockhead´s are evo) And an Ironhead looks like a shovelhead unless you know the difference. The Ironhead sportster has 2 different style of rocker boxes, but why call it an Ironhead, because between 1957 to 1985 1/2 these were made with Cast Iron heads, where as the other´s I mention have aluminum heads.

author: NN
text: Please advise me on proper rear chain adjustment.  Any hints on the adjustment would be appreciated.

author: Lee
text: Proper adjustment is 1/2 inch free up and down movement in center of slack side of chain.  Be sure to measure both sides of axle when retightening to maintain wheel alingment.

author: Hippie Dave
text: Chain adjustment should be checked with the weight of the rider in the seat since the swingarm position changes with the weight of the rider. You should also check it in more than one spot on the chain since chains can be tighter and/or looser in different positions as they age.

author: Olen Wood
text: I want to convert my sporty to electric start. Can this be done,and if so what parts are needed. I know I need a sprocket cover that is notched for the starter and a clutch drum with a starter ring gear,but what else. Any help will be welcome. I´m getting too old to keep pumping on this beast and just want to ride these day. Besides it very embarassing to run out of energy and have some young dude ask if he can start it for me at my local biker bar.

author: dennycee
text: Yeah, It can be done. A buddy and I did it it to my ´72 eight years ago. Near-complete kits are available. Everything you described is true, but take a look at your sprocket cover. On the back may already be the outline for the notch you need to cut. A high-speed cutoff wheel will do it, might save a couple $$. Also watch out for the cheezy handlebar switches in the kit. I´ve seen ´em short against the bars if you don´t solder them just so and use tape or rtv. You will also need to replace your battery, battery box and oil tank. Electric start battery is huge compared to stock kicker battery. These DID NOT come in the kit, so you´ll be checking out the parts boxes in some old coots´ garage. Also, a little looking at the swap meets found me a clutch drum with the starter gear already on it.Try to go for the heaviest starter relay and solenoid you can, as I had a few problems with them sticking. Good Luck. I´ll check back. -Den

author: NN
text: i just got rid of both side covers and the starter in trade for other parts but yes they can be cut out with care .we put the starter button on a mount just below the seat out of the way but easy to reach .shorter wires = less drag!!good luck   headless.

author: Tom Burke
text: Any good after market shop can provide all the parts you need.  I assume you are not an experienced mechanic or you would not be asking this question.  It isn´t a real complicated process but if you are not an experienced mechanic, pay somebody to do it for you.  The teeth you save could be your own. A word of caution though.  If you are working with the stock frame, the longer forks will compromise the turning radius and the stability of the bike.  It will also tend to amplify any vibration coming from the front wheel. I had mine 6 inches over stock for a while but I went back to stock length and I am glad I did.  It is a much smoother ride at stock.

author: Den
text: Joe, If you can find it, in american Iron mag from Aug 2002 there is an article of electric start in the Xlch. Its not as difficult as it seems. Apparently there is room without to many changes. XCLH came after the XLH series CH stood for Competition Hot actual Title was to be Competition High-compression. 1977 was the last year of the XLCH. I also have a customized 77XLCH and have often dreamt of an electric start, But every year in the spring after a few kicks I forget about it and ride. Hope this helps. Chrome side up. Den

author: Peter
text: The XLH & XLCH both had kick start up until either ´67 or ´68.  Then the XLH got the electric starter while the XLCH stayed with the kick starter.  Because the XLH had the electric starter it also had a larger battery.  The larger battery partly hung out of the frame as did the oil tank.  The XLCH´s battery and oil tank were inside the frame.  On the XLH the battery dug into the riders left leg and the oil tank burned his right leg when stopped.

text: thanks for the help but as it turns out, i finally figured it out.  a couple of years ago a guy re-built the bottom end and it seems as though for some un-known reason he did´nt use the factory timing mark on the flywheel.  instead he made his own mark and it was´nt a problem until i slung out the timing advance weights and had to replace them.  needless to say, i could´nt get it timed right.  i finally noticed the homemade mark, tried it and it worked.  go figure!  anyway, all of this has convinced me to spend the monet and go to an electronic ignition.  thanx again, maybe i can help someone some time.  c-ya.

author: NN
text: I´m getting vibration at 60 MPH and over on my ´73 Ironhead .  Has anyone got a cure for this.

author: Tom Burke
text: Start with getting a good spin balance on both front and back wheels.  This could reduce the vibration significantly. The engine is bolted solidly to the frame and it is chain drive.  It will never ride smoothly because of this design.  There is nothing to insulate the vibration from the frame.  Make sure the chain and sprockets are in good condition and adjusted to spec too. Also, if the front end is longer than stock, go back to the stock length.  The longer fork tubes tend to amplify the vibration. I bought mine new in 1973 so I have tried a few things over the years.  Some things worked and some didn´t. Good luck!

author: Ironhead 73
text: Get used to it.  The motor is not rubber mounted so this will add a little vibration.  How is the front end of the bike?  Is the triple tree secured?  Sometimes that can make a difference.

author: tony
text: rear brake light does not work need help please

author: adamdinat
text: There are a couple op problem areas to check. I had trouble with the tail light socket getting sloppy and ended up replacing that. Check the tail light using the front brake. Another spot to check is the connections to the brake light switch. It is in a spot where it collects lots of dirt and may be corroded.  I had to cut back into the harness to get good wire and put on new lugs. Check up under the fender to be sure the harness has not been caught on the tire and dammaged. Do you have a wiring diagram for this bike?  I can scan mine and send it to you if you need one.

text: I have a wiring diagram if you still need it. I bought my ´79 XLS new 28 years ago and just found this Web site.

author: Habiru
text: Check to ensure that the bulb is not burned out, and that all of the wires are intact. Next, look on the frame behind the rear cylinder and you should see the brake light sending unit mounted there. If need be, follow the brake line from the rear master cylinder to the sending unit. There should be two wires going to the sending unit. One should be hot when you turn the key on, and the other should show hot when you apply pressure to the rear brake pedal. If this doesnt happen, your sending unit (pressure switch) is bad.

author: Rich Kuechenmeister
text: Vin on a stock frame is located on the Right side just below the steering head.  There will be a flat section approximated 2 inches long, about an 1/2 inhc wide.  On original stock bike the VIN on the frame and on the engine is the same.  On the engine look right side below the carborator on the lower end.  It will start with an H2 ###### whatever.

author: NN
text: it is on the r front neck cast and should also be between the cylinders on the block

author: NN
text: Check the VIN #. First number is ´Made in USA´, then HD, then a single #, then the MODEL DESIGNATION.  CA = XLH, CBH = XLS, CC = XLX, and CDH = XR-1000.  Incidentally, the last letter in the VIN signifies the Plant of Manufacture. (my ´82 Sportster is Y for York) hope that helps.
