text: I owned a Cb450 nighthawk a few years ago and it was a great bike,light,good mileage. I am now looking to buy a new small motorcycle and am looking at the CB250 nighthawk as Honda no longer makes the CB450. I would like to hear from anyone who owns one and what they have to say.

author: rahavens
text: dear regular guy: i have read tests,and stastics on the cb250. If you wish me to compare it to youre 450, here goes. While the 450 is strong enough for 2 up freeway riding, the 250 is not. You can ride it it solo on the freeway (is does not have power for the diamond lane). The 250 gets 100 miles per gallon. It can carry a passenger in town (as long as the tripp does not exceed 10 miles). The cb250 has the greatest safety record og all motorcycles in the united states) The cb250 is the finest economy bike on the market.

author: Jeff
text: I´m troubled by the Nighthawk´s low gear ratio. At 35mph I´m already shifting into 5th gear. For this reason I´ve never taken my bike onto the freeway. I´m considering either a smaller primary sprocket (1 to 2 teeth reduction) or a larger secondary sprocket. Has anyone else tried this approach?

author: richard havens
text: Jeff:There is a 30 tooth back sprocket which will gain you five mph crusing speed. what you also need is an electronic tachometer which is aviable for that bike. During break in do not exceed 4500 rpm during break in. afterwards do not exceed 5500 rpm. It would after break in be freeway safe if;you stay in the slow lane, do not try it with a passenger, and do not haul more than a tankbag full of baggage.

author: The Inspector
text: I increased the front sprocket from 14 to 15 teeth. Increased top speed and ease of cruising @ 65-70.  I have ridden on the highway over 100 miles at a time, and will be doing some 3 to 400 mile tours this summer. Great bike with great mileage. Added foam grips and a gel seat for comfort.

author: Ron
text: I bought the honda 250 Nighthawk and it is a great bike if you are riding solo.  I cant put the bike down for what it is but I am used to larger bikes.  Figure top speed to be between 70-75 mph depending on the highway grade.  Handlebar virations are aweful so a larger, smoother, lower reving engine would be more comfortable.  however it is sprinty and comfortable considering its size.  Mpg is super high.  I get 80.545 for that last two months use.  i want a goldwing but this gets me around.

author: Edison
text: I own this bike for over then three years and I am very satisfied with it.Lately I use super 98 octan(no lead) petrol and I sense more power from engine.During this time I didn´t have any problem with engine,except with some electrical parts(turn signalization relay,speedo instrument)which is probably due to long time use.Occassionally I do trips for about 500 km longs,and never left me down.I like it very much driving it and because of this bike now I´m hooked to Honda bikes.Honda rules!!! P.S. Honda cb 250 rs will allways be my nr.1 bike!

author: Crick
text: I ran a Honda 250 RS for about 4 years before I finally had enough of the continual stator problems. I have not noticed anyone else experiencing this problem, so my bike might have some other issues, perhaps a bent crank. But I want to say that I had a ball on this bike. Economical, low to the ground, reliable (apart from that one problem). It´s not good at anything, speed, handling, braking, but it is all good enough if you to ride as a commuter or tourist. I rode Melbourne to Queensland, 2800 kilometers for $80 of fuel. To me, that´s fun! Tyres are cheap and it was reliable...except for you know what. I now ride a TT 350 Yamaha dirt bike. Totally different experience. Rode a friends Honda 750. Totally different again. And now I´m considering putting the RS back on the road! Have to solve stator issues though.

author: Whiteboy Bob
text: Has anyone ever tried mating a 250 Nighthawk engine to a 250 Kawasaki Ninja transmission?  Would that improve top end/crusing speed or would I have to tweak the engine as well?

author: Hawk
text: You don´t need to change trannys to get highway speed.  just drop a tooth or two on the rear sprocket.  That will drop your RPMS and keep your engine alive longer.  Check out the CB250 Yahoo group.  They have lots of free speed mods for the hawk that will help you keep up with the larger bikes.

author: Steve
text: If you have the money to think about adding a ninja tranny you really should be looking for a new bike. I would just change the sprocket and maybe add some pods to it. Also just making sure you have correct tire pressure helps increase the top end. It´s not much obviously but it´s definitely worth it, keep the tire from uneven wear and prevents a blow out.

author: hutch
text: Just recently got my certification and I only have about 2 days exp. riding so far. Been looking at a few bikes and for the money this seems like a good starter bike. I would like to get a 600-750, but all the ones I have seen are a little out of my price range right now. My only question about this bike is, can it sustain speeds of about 65-70mph on the interstate without putting serious strain on the engine? Thanks for any feedback.

author: Paul
text: So long as you don´t go over the red line and keep the oil changed at least to schedule (I change mine more often) you can go as fast as you want for as long as the police and your wallet permit. Think of tire condition, condition of brakes, oil changes, condition of drive chain, etc.  That is, think preventive maintenance.  Don´t think of "strain," it simply isn´t an issue.  You can go 500,000 miles on that engine easy if you take care of things.  However, if you let your drive chain deteriorate to the point that it breaks and whips around and whacks a hole in your transmission or engine case, that´s as far as you go until you fix it. Paul

author: Steve
text: You don´t really have to worry about over reving the bike in 5th though. The air resistance is going to put enough strain on the bike that it will just stop accelerating and not over rev. If you´ve only got 2 days riding exp. Don´t get a 600. I work at a dealer/repair shop and we refer to riders who buy bigger bikes as \

author: NN
text: i´ve been riding my 78 for a few years now and love it for its reliability and low maintainence costs, but the only thing it lacks is the fact that it can only sit on around 80-90km/h for extended periods of time. does anyone have any ideas to increase its top speed? sincerly happy honda owner

author: Yo bro wassap
text: The cheapest and easiest way is to change the size of the gear wheel front (make larger) or rear (make smaller) .  Although you can increase top speed, your power output on the rear wheel will be adversely affected. Also, a higher top speed also means increased drag. The only thing that can overcome increased drag in a fixed design is an increase in power.(Which you don´t have.) Try a 400 cc engine:-)

author: Yo bro wassap
text: Did I mention that changing the chain gears (to increase top speed) also puts higher stress on bearings and transmission?

text: i just baought a nighthawk 250 last saterday. i dont have my permit yet (too young by 5 months) so i have been just riding around in the grass. the acceleration doesnt feel too bad. and it starts up wonderfully even in the cold. the only thing is it takes a little to warm up. pulling in the clucth is a little wierd if you are used to dirtbikes. you dont have to pull the clutch in even half way, and you have a little free play (naturally)but a little more than usual, so it feels alittle odd for a little bit. the seat position is comfortable though might be a bit cramped on long trips for me i am 5´10". i havent ridden it enough to tell if it is a bit cramped. tell you wen i have it on the road. doesnt have the best acceleration, but you can still tell when it accelerates, you can feel a slight pull on your arms. REAL fun to ride, not as fun as dirtbiking but still fun. tell you more later

author: Rolf
text: Hi, You don´t have to worry about this comfortable position during long trips. I have my bike (CB250 Nighthawk 1993) from 3 years and made many long trips - about 2000km (1200 miles) during vacations. There is no problem to make 400 km (250 miles) daily, using second class roads. This is a city motorcycle but you can use it for turistic as well if you want. The only problem is akceleration. You need realy long free space to precede a truck going 90 km/h (55m/h).

author: Phill
text: This comment on the Honda CB250RS is quite true. I have one myself that I bought while I was saving up to get a 1000cc rocket machine. I still have the 250 because it blows my mind with its overall performance. I honestly doubt that there are any road bikes as reliable and as durable as the Honda CB250RS. It can do 160kph, wheelstand up the road 1st and 2nd gear, not bad for little guy with one pot and 24 years behind it, plus I also found it to be very excellent off road as well, taking jumps, slides, and power drift in gravell and dirt, best fun I have had on any motorcycle far better than any Harley that has ever been made.

author: NN
text: I bought this bike in late 2000, and have had it since (2002). I live in Holland, and have been to Belgium, England, France, Luxembourg and Germany with it in the summer of 2001. The bike is really reliable, although there are some minor problems. The most anoying was the braking problemn, escpecially if the original hydrolic rubber tube is still fitted, you get slugish braking, and you can pull the brake lever all the way back, untill it bumps your steeringgrip. I solved the problem by fitting a stainless-steel tube (40 Euro), and now it brakes like never before. An other big problem is on the rear wheel. There is a ditch for fixing the rear chain-gear with a circlip, which is almost always worn out on all bikes. That results in a lot of clearance on the rear chain-gear, and eventually braking loose. The only option is a new rearwheel. But on overall, the bike is great fun to ride, but don´t count on big performance, especially not on the highway. The maximum speed without frontwind is about 110 kmh. If you have winds, then sometimes you don´t reach above 85-90 kmh.

author: Miguel Rosselló
text: Hallo, I have a problem with my front brake. I dont speak and write so much english but... when I have my BMW R-65 1985 in the sun the front brake close. What can I do?. Cuando tengo mi BMW R-65 del año 1985 al sol, se dilata algo en el freno delantero y me bloquea la rueda, hasta que no se enfría (por no estar al sol) no se afloja. He ido a varios mecánicos y no saben el porque. Alguien puede ayudarme. Gracias.

author: Rob
text: Flush out old brake fluid, fill and bleed with new fluid. Find the bleeding procedure. You will have a difficult time if you do not bleed correctly.

author: karim
text: It sounds like your brake is going into hydrostatic lock due thermal expansion because the feed port from the reservoir to the master cylinder is possibly blocked not allowing fluid to go back into reservoir when brake lever is released . Get a syringe with a clear plastic/rubber pipe fill it with DOT 3 brake fluid ,connect it to bleed nipple on caliper ,crack nipple open and pressurize using syringe. If the reservoir does not fill up, the master cylinder is at fault (piston is blocking supply/fluid return passage to reservoir or foreign matter is blocking orifice. Pull master cylinder apart and investigate. Start dis- assembly by removing reservoir and you will see the port in question. If it is covered up when brake lever is in relaxed position then there is your problem.You will have to investigate further by complete dis-assembly.

author: Jason DiNicola
text: This was my first bike bought in 97´ with about 30K miles on it. The bike was basically whipped, but I managed to put 13K miles on it for a few seasons it suddenly lost power after some strange noises came out of the engine area. The bike was not fast, didn´t particularly brake very well, but it´s handling wasn´t too bad for what it was. It was a very flickable and you could throw the thing right into a corner quite nicely and it would ride the line just fine and solid....that is until you hit a bump, when the bike would dance beneath you. Not exactly confidence inspiring for sporting onto new roads. The bike was loud too....it made alot of noise, but never really went anywhere that fast. I used to keep the amble amount of chrome on the bike shining and looking good. I got plenty of great remarks on how good it looked and how it was great to see an old classic still on the road. I was proud to ride that little bike. The greatest thing about this motorcycle was the fact you never got caught in the performance of the machine, but rather the lack of performance as you putted throughout the country forced you to take more enjoyment from the scenery than from cutting apexes. Mine is still in storage awaiting restoration. Buy one if you can.

text: hi from New Zealand.i have a 1968 cb450 k1.its the early model with small triangular badges.not a lot were produced before parts were superceeded into the more common k1 with the larger DOHC450 triangular badges.ive noted at least 7 variations of k1 models which were made till late 1969 when the K2 painted tank model without knee pads appeared.anyone interested in detail changes can contact me.as an on going quality control measure parts were superceeded continuously throughout production runs.honda was very thorough in this field.id be pleased to assist anyone wanting technical information and share pics of our lovely 5 speed k1s.regards Graeme Duckett.New Zealand

author: Tony
text: This is one of my favourite bikes. I bought a K1 second hand in 1972 and loved it. The engine is reliable (and very sophisticated with the DOHC & torsionon bar valve springs). Handling is good for a Honda of the period, it does scrape and the tall engine makes for a high centre of gravity. Replacing the shocks with Konis and heavier fork oil helps. It has buckets of torque and pulls like a train. In its day (pre CB750) it was the flagship of Honda´s range and the first Japanese bike to earn real respect. (Suzuki 500´s were 2 stroke and didn´t quite have what we´d now call street-cred)

author: Cas
text: I drove one of these for 13 years, from about 1983 to 1996.  It was a 1966 model and ended up with 122,000 miles on it!!  From about 96,000 onwards the damn thing broke my heart, mostly because of really poor quality parts from Honda themselves.  And I had only rebuilt it at that mileage as a precaution against mechanical failure!!! The list of ill fitting/ poor quality parts includes, valve guides that did not fit, and torsion bars with forks that would not fit around the valve stems.  Two of these torsion bars did not have properly treated springs, and after about 3000 miles the valves were not closing properly, causing the bike to hole a piston.  I put the old ones back in and persevered for a few miles yet, but no longer had enough confidence in the bike to constantly rev it to the limit as before.  The fun was gone, and when the carbs eventually started to break up, I gave up. Before that it was good cheap fun.  Jap classic enthusiasts who, in my experience much prefer to polish than to ride, also put me off big time.

author: har gopal
text: They usually stop making parts for most bikes including bikes that you could go buy at the dealer today a year or 2 after the bikes are produced,  I was also told that parts are only availabel for motorcycles because not all the pikes were assembled.  So basically there are only parts because they didn´t sell as many bikes as they intended to so the unassembled bikes get sold as part and when they are gone thay are gone.  Bikes vary so much from year to year that the ffactories are completely retooled and wheb that happens it isn´t cost effective to retool a machine in order to make old parts.  Still for these older bikes ebay is a great place to look and right now there is a guy who has what looks like 2 71 cb450´s in parts for sale right now.  Other than that there is a web site called pro source at www.shop.prosaourceoutlet.com that has all the avilable parts for these bikes as far as I can tell.  Good luck and keep these great machines running.

text: Find the bike very temperamental and difficult to start. My carburetors seem to be leaking. I find when the tank is half full, it seem´s impossible to start again if it stalls, and will only start if I refill the tank to full. When it is misty or rainy, it seems not to get any gas, and I am unable to go anywhere?  I am trying to find a repair or service manual, but BMW  here in Edmonton, Alberta, Canada, tell me that the BMW here is not the same as the Russian BMW and so cannot guarantee that a service manual for a 650 cc bike from here may not be the same or be guaranteed? I am not familiar with these bikes, so therefore do not know where to find fuel filters, etc. Should I dismantle my carbs and fuel lines, and is it difficult to find parts for this bike if necessary? Is there any ongoing maintenance for these bikes that I should know about? I just discovered tonight that the shaft that goes through my rear wheel was almost having the nut that holds it on about to come off! (Just a few threads holding it on) After tightening, it seemed difficult to go, I couldn´t tell if it was because the bike was not getting it´s gas (although it seemed that way?) or whether by tightening that bolt on the right side of the rear wheel I may have obstructed it´s ability to drive? My red light on the front right kept flashing on, and as far as I can tell my oil appears to be proper. Should I be using a 10/40 oil for this bike? Also, there is something that looks like an electric starter motor above and in front of the cylinders.Did this bike come with an electric start, or is this just some kind of generator? I just bought the bike 3 weeks ago, and the weather has been too lousy to really study this bike. I also do not have a garage, so I am at the mercy of the elements. If anyone can give me any information as to all of these concerns, or knowledge as to where I can get a repair manual and or parts when needed (including costs) I would be very much appreciative!!   W. Warren Milley  Phone: (780)469-6570 or write to 9624-99 A Street, Edmonton, Alberta, Canada. T6E 3W8

author: ulbruce
text: I had an ´06 Dyna Low Rider with a Stage I and it was a great running bike until I started having some electrical issues at 38,000 miles. I now have a ´15 Low Rider with the much bigger engine and also a well tuned Stage I. The bike has tons of torque, especially over 3000 R.P.M., but it doesn´t seem to be as much fun to ride. Maybe I need to get used to it and keep it revved higher, but I´m consistently getting 45-46 MPG and that´s almost 10 MPG better than I used to get. I´m not complaining by any means, it just seems the 103 moves the bike around so effortlessly and I feel like I could cruise around all day in 4th or 5th gear. I don´t even think about going into 6th until I´m over 60 MPH....

author: CanadianFXLRSGuy
text: No more Dynas now. My 2021 Low Rider is a Softail. My only Dyna was a 2010 Super Glide which is very similar to the Dyna version of the Low Rider. I enjoyed that bike very much but traded it in for a 2014 Street Glide in September of 2013 which I later paid off and traded in for a brand new 39 HP farm tractor with loader and snowblower to save my back for snow removal at our new property where I could park three tractor trailers with 53´ trailers attached end to end easily and my other driveway which is more of a parking lot in front of my garage which I could easily park four tractor trailers with the previously mentioned configuration side by side and two deep. All that with Degenerative Disk Disease does not make for fun shovelling and we´ve got farm animals to look after. But, besides all that, I´m finally able to afford another Harley-Davidson after almost another four years. What I´ve come to realize in that time is that selling my last bike was the worst thing I could have ever done for PTSD recovery. I guess my point is, if you´ve got a Harley, hold on to it for dear life. It´ll keep you sane.

text: No it will not.  The ´84 frame was for the shovelhead.  In ´85 they changed the backbone of the frame and the triangular area between the seat and the tank was welded in for more support for the evo motor.  The mounting tab for the seats therefore changed.

text: Anybody who can help me with this please email me. I have had this bike since 1983, a year ago an establishment worked on my transmittion and then it started leaking, a transmittion seal.Two failed attempts I placed in another establishment for repair, only to be disappointed two days later with leaking.Both certified mechanics, have never leaked, always been a great ride. Help get me out of the pool of fluid .

author: Greif
text: I am guessing that you are leaking from the main shift coming from the transmission. If so there is a main shaft oil seal part # 37741-82 and then there is according to the parts book a seal that belongs to the primary housing that has a part number of 12018. The oil seal for the main shaft does not require that the transmission is taken off the bike it can be replaced very simply with a screw driver however, always use a rubber mallet and a piece of pipe the same diameter as the seal. I always find that sometimes the bike has some 1984 parts. I believe this has to do with the month the bike was built at least in my case. So, the primary housing might have a part number of 12052.

author: Bruno C

author: carbie
text: I had an 84 FXSB shovel early model kick start. I had some carb problems that resembled electrical problems that took a while to diagnose. However I worked on it for hours and got it running beautifully. I rode the s**t out of this bike including trips to sturgis and laconia. It had a true harley sound and I had many compliments from bikers from everywhere. This was not a bike for the lighthearted weekend warrior. It rattled most fillings out of my mouth and required constant wrenching after long rides. But it was all worth it when I kicked that bike over and heard the potato masher swing into gear. This is Harley at its best. An old school dream. It is one great ride.

author: Dick Logan
text: I have a 1980 lower rider.  10,000 miles Excellent condition.  All of my turn signals went out at the same time. Bulbs are good  No power to any of them.  Otherwise no electrical problems.  Wher should I look

author: Reverend KP
text: Look in the headlight. There´s an orange wire that powers the turn signal relay, that in turn powers all four turn signals. The constant jarring has evidently loosened up the connection. It should be easy to trace with a meter. See if you´ve got power to the relay and back check it to the accessories circuit breaker on the rear fender under the seat.

author: Tom Buffalo NY
text: Well, took it back to the Buffalo Harley Dealer an had the Clutch adjusted to my liking an told them to check the timing. You think the money I paid to do this job they be better at it. One, it fouled out when I first got it, an to my horror, when, I say TRIED at frist to take the rear spark plug out, who ever crank, got it so tight they had to take into the shop to get it out. Back to today, they found the timing was off, someone forgot to tighten the backing plate so it was loose. Not the end of the story, pulled away an 10 minutes latter had to come back to the shop for it ran even worse. They pulled it in an 20 minutes later said I had to leave it, for BOTH plugs where black. So, something with the carb an could be the float, or, since it was sitting so long, I wonder if it be a good idea to soak the carb in gas to clean it out. Glad it was running like crap near the shop rather some where for a ride in the jungle. LOL.     Any comments welcomed. Tom Tomflorkowski@aol.com

author: clove
text: Is the battery supposed to pull down when you turn on the ignition?

author: Reverend KP
text: I´m not sure I understand what you mean by pull down your battery. If you mean the motor turns slowly when you hit the start button, then the problem could be that your brushes are nearly worn out in the starter. Another answer might be a weak cell in the battery where the starter doesn´t get the full voltage necessary to turn the starter over at full strength. In the later case, start with a battery with a full charge of 12 plus volts.

author: Tom Florkowski
text: thanks for the response. I went to my dearler an he did not have the FSXB inner primarcy case PN # 60504-84. He said he did a dealer serch an came up with nothing. But, I happen to come up with a phone number by chance, an called Wesellcycles.com (1-814-317-7035) ask for Vince an he gets wrecked bikes an has a network, an did a serch himself an came up with the inner primary case he told me from a Harley Dealer that was on the shelf. So, beginning to wonder how good Buffalo Harley Davidson looked like he did. Maybe they where pushing me into buying a new bike. ( just a thought, but, funny he found it.) Your comments on this be interesting I tried Vparts.com for after market an they did not have it. Called JP Cycles.com an talked to their Harley Techie an he said he has one in black. But, this could be for a Sturgis with belt drive because they had black cases, but, I could be wrong ??? I was told it had to be for a FXE or FXSB. Softtail would not be a option. Being a shovel head bottom end, why is it so difficult to find a inner primary case ( 60504-84) for my 1985 FXSB? Has it something to do with a bolting partern to the Evo Case? I had a suggestion of welding the case with maybe two into one. I never thought about keeping the broken part at the time to be honest for never thought about welding. And the case looks like it is crack by a bolt hole or two from what I can remember. But, with that said, If you had to make two into one, any suggestions how to go about it for my 1985 FXSB? Could I take a Shovel inner primary an do any machine work to make it work with what I have. Tom Buffalo NY Tomflorkowski@aol.com PS keep this site in mind, Wesellcycles.com for they collect lots of parts for recycling to older bikes.

author: Rich
text: Not sure if you solved your inner primary trouble. I have a ´79 FL and a ´85FXSB I know all the mounting bolts are the same to motor and tranny. There is one extra bolt on the outer cover on the bottom under the clutch on the ´85. The difference is in the starter. I put a shovel outer primary on the 85 inner primary, it bolted up except for the starter bearing boss in the outer primary. I machined the boss shorter and reinstalled the bearing. My wife´s been running it for years.

author: Buelligan
text: I just purchased a used 1993 HD FXRC Lowrider Convertible. I like the pop off windshield, it will come in handy this winter, and I like the saddlebag design. Easy on and off. While the bike doesn´t have as much power as I´m used to, it does seem to move along quite well. I like the dual discs up front, as most of your braking power is there. It does take alot to slow down 650lbs of steel. It´s got decent ground clearance and handles quite well for a Harley. I do plan on changing out the gas tank, and moving the gauges down to the tank one of these days, I´d like to clean up the front of the bike and it looks like the stock gauge location was an afterthought. I´ve got the Corbin seat, and it´s REAL comfortable. For tires, I run the Pirelli Diablo Corsas as they provide good traction and decent mileage. I also run these on one of my Buells.

author: Baxtah
text: Hello... brand new 05 dyna lowrider owner here. Very excited about the up coming spring. I loved reading everyones comments on this great machine. But I have one problem that I would like to through out there. The kick stand required special care. I have heard horror stories about fallen machines. I have addressed this with my selling dealer... who has seen a few bikes crash to the floor on the show room floor. my salesman suggestedto roll back on the bike to be sure the kick stand does not move backward and fail. I´ve been told this on other harley models.  My father... also a dyna low rider owner... uses a bungy that goes around the forward peg, and ont the loop in the kick stand... he says that what he saw when he visited fulerton Harley Davidson in California. has anyone else had trouble with this kick stand set up? any stronger kicks stands avalible in the after market?

author: fxdlidon
text: Hi, had mine for just about a year now and have never had a problem even since I´ve fitted shorter rear shocks (originals rotted out!) which should make make the problem even worse. Just have to be careful every time you park up: lean the bike slightly away from the stand, kick it hard forward, then rest the bike on it. I normaly drag the bike back slightly to make sure the stand´s locked in place. Just a habit you need to get into. Oh... and keep the mechanism well lubed - tends to pick up a lot of road dirt from the front wheel.

author: NN
text: In 1981 HD produced the low rider FXS and the sturgis FXB.  The FXB was black, had drag bars and twin belts.  The FXS was chain drive, both primary and final, and had pull back bars.  Both bikes had the 80" motor that was standard on the big twins.  Compression was 7.4:1.  There was no anniversary model.

author: NN
text: What an incredible machine. Rode a 95 1500 vulcan 88 for six years and traded for a 02 fxdl dyna lowrider. There is no comparison. The twin cam 88 feels nearly as powerful as the twin plug/twin carb. vulcan with better fuel mileage. the balance and stability is probably the best of any cruiser I have ever ridden (with the exception of the v-rod). Fit and finish were outstanding and visually the lowrider has always been a great looking machine. I would highly recommend this motorcycle to anyone and feel it is the most versitle of all the newer HD bikes.

author: Husky rider
text: This is really crazy, I bought a brand new XR400 this spring a rode it all summer in sand, track, serious enduro and thought that it was the final offroad solution. However I took I ride at my old Husky 400WR from 1986 and found it better in all environments. The first gear is made for really smoth slow almost trial conditions and second for normal difficult slow enduro challanges but when you came out on open country the Husky never stop wheeling not even on 4 gear..... My conclution is that the only development that have happen since 1986 is the electric starter (not even on the XR). The Husky 400 -86 was the final solution.

author: Diz.
text: Please help! This began just yesterday. I get power when turning the ignition on, but when pressing the start button.... nothing, not even a click. I´ve checked fuses, and wiring that I can reach without pulling the whole thing apart. I gave the starter a little tap. I pulled the starter switch apart and cleaned it up, checked the contact on the clutch lever and still nothing. The only thing I haven´t done is hooked up a volt meter, because I don´t have one.. That´s the next step. If you can give any more pointers I would greatly appreciate it. Thanks. Diz.

author: Herb
text: I would check the starter solenoid (behind the left frame cover) and the wiring to the kill switch and starter button (at the right hand grip).  Look for dirty/corroded connections on the wiring leading to all of these points.

author: Brad Gunn
text: I have an older bike the gr 650 1985 its been a good bike I have approximately 50,000 kms on it so far but one problem I have found it I am not getting the mileage I should out of the bike I travelled today approximately 160kms on one tank of gas which is 2 and a half gallons of gas I think I should be getting better mileage, I had the carberateurs cleaned within the last two years and they were very clean inside no varnish build up, the bike is in need of new seals as I top the oil up every 200km on trips as it leaks oils around the seals of the base of the engine and the top of the engine, I was told this would not have much effect on the bike, I keep my chain tight and pressure in my tires correct in order to preserve fuel and dont drive faster than 110km on the highway, my exhaust pies don´t leak, so the engine pressure should be the same, maybey I am to expect this type of fuel mileage out of my bike, the engine has not been opened up since I have owned so I could not tell you what the inside is like, I find that on warm day of 15 degrees celcius I have to open the throttle to warm it up for at least thirty seconds, I also checked the fuel lines for leaks haven´t found anything and the bike burns clean out the exhaust  any body have the same problems  Brad thanks

author: 1001davidstone
text: how did your restoration go?  I bought a running, clean, 83 tempter 3 months ago. ran wonderfully, but unknown to me the cam chain was worn and loose. it broke last month, and is now in pieces being repaired. I am finding certain, model specific, parts are not readily available.  I am looking into a 1989 gs500 cam chain guide as a hopeful fit to my 650. I have heard the engines are the same. author: NN
text: I´ve owned this bike for nearly two months now, and it´s excellent. I just thought I should post a note about the one problem I have had. If you´re bike is stalling when you knock it into gear, it is probably because your bike THINKS the stand is down,.. the reason being.. behind the top of the stand is a small plug. Take that apart and you will find a small notch should fit into an even smaller hole. If this is not catching, that is the reason.

author: New Hampshire
text: This is a review of the twin, not the single shown in the pictures.  This is a smooth twin 800cc engine with a six speed transmission. It can carry enough cargo to serve as a mid weight adventure tourer. It is nimble on the pavement and handles well off road. You won’t go where dirt bi,es can go, but you shouldn’t expect to. It is great on gas, getting around 65 MPG of regular, not super.

author: Tim
text: This is the review of the twin, not the single. This is a great all around bike, very smooth on the highway at speed and nimble around twisties.  It´s not a hard-core off-roader at 450 pounds, but it can do light off-road.  The fuel economy is great at 60-70 mpg depending on driving habits, and it takes regular.  Add the optional luggage and it is a mid-weight adventure bike. I haven´t had it long enough to gauge the cost factor; I assume that since it´s a BMW it won´t be cheap.

author: CA
text: Hi all. I needed a commuter bike (my restored ´76 Kwaka Z1000 required a step back from the daily mail run) that i could have fun on in the weekend and tour on. After reading old mags and online reviews I narrowed down a handful and the F650 was the first to become available in my area. I have only had the sexy beast two weeks and have loved every minute on it, it devours the tarmac and surprises the hell out of trail riders on the dirt and metal tracks running alongside the Waimakariri (largest braided river in the South Island, New Zealand). Mind you it surprised the hell out of me on a few of the slower sections when an unusual whirring suddenly kicked in making me think i had pushed it a bit far. Having only ridden air cooled bikes before I didn´t immediately recognise the sound of a radiator fan! Which brings me to a question, obviously i´m not the sharpest knife in the rack, but i´ve looked hard, and damned if i can find any sort of gauge or window which lets me keep track of coolant level? So i jump on the net and google for an owners manual, download a complete copy and discover its in German. Pleasantly suitable but beyond my talents... Repeated attempts have resulted in nothing but frustration, even pages that supposedly only have advice on day to day maintenance lead to nothing more than commercial booksellers or error messages. I´m all for superiority but someone please tell me English hasn´t surrendered to Deutsch efficiency as far as information flow on the www goes?! Where can i download the info i need to look after my bike? From what i´ve experienced so far it´s worth looking after. Loving it.

author: David P
text: I bought a new 05 F650GS Dakar about 2 weeks ago and now have over 400 miles on it. I´m quite happy with it so far, both on and off road. It has lots of power and a comfortable ride. BMW should add a sixth gear for more relaxed highway cruising. For low speed/technical riding off road you may want to drop one or two teeth on the front sprocket as 1st and 2nd gear are somewhat tall. I´ve heard the engine smooths out after some break-in time but there is a noticeable buzz felt in the bars and foot pegs between 4700-5200rpm (72-78mph). It feels better at 80mph and above. The break-in has been more agressive and my gas mileage has been 60-75mpg. I´ve heard this improves with mileage. The bike will easily hit 100mph. The speedo and odometer appear very close to accurate specs. For me, the seat is comfortable for only about an hour on the highway, and more so if I slide back to the wider/thicker part of the seat. I´m 5´11"/170 and get the toes of both feet on the ground at stops. While seated upright the airflow off the windscreen hits me in the upper chest which leaves my helmet in clean air. Highway wind buffeting is not bad to 70mph but much worse in the vicinity of large vehicles. For long distance highway rides I´ll obtain a larger windscreen that can be switched in a matter of minutes. The brakes/ABS work well but I´ve noticed some rear brake squeal at times. The heated hand grips work and should be nice when colder weather comes around. From my experience (and many others), the bike will run fine or even better on 87 octane fuel and the dealers can even program the bikes computer for this if it´s not already standard. BMW wants you to bring the bike in for all scheduled services and will check the computer for anything that may develop into a problem later. I believe the warranty is 3 years/36,000 miles with roadside assistance is included. If you didn´t know, the fuel tank is actually under the seat (nice) and the exhaust is not dual, it crosses over and exits out the right side (left side is sealed). The exhaust is fairly quiet, especially at a moderate cruising speed. I´ve had no problems with the Dakar and get positive comments about it´s appearance.

author: David P
text: The Dakar now has over 900 miles. I made a 350 mile round-trip last weekend for the 600 mile run-in service. Cost was $193 for parts/labor. I´ve installed a Wunderlich Ergo tall windscreen and cruising at 70-90mph is now comfortable. There is slight buffeting on the upper arms and the airflow hits near the top of my helmet. Ducking my head 3-4" is much quieter but not comfortable. Raising my head makes no difference which tells me the short screen may have worked well enough. I easily see over the top of this tall screen with a view of the ground less than 20´ ahead. I had the speedometer checked and found it reads 3mph fast at 60-80. Cruising at an indicated 80 (77 actual) got me 53mpg on the first leg of my weekend trip. Doing 85 (82) resulted in 55mpg. The bike cruises effortlessly over 80. In coming weeks I expect to make a 1,200 mile trip which will take me above 8,000-9,000´ at times. The overall speed will be less and I expect mileage will improve. A Cortech tank bag and tail bag hold enough gear/clothes for 3 days and attach easily to the bike. No problems with the bike so far.

author: Rubber side down
text: Hi! Although this isn´t a solution for a better windscreen, you can dampen the buffeting and turbulance by seating yourself in an aggressive forward leaning position at higher speeds.  This will position your helmet closer to the windscreen, allowing more air deflection to pass over your helmet as opposed to hitting it full face (no pun intended).  You´ll notice how quiet your bike is and how noisy the wind can be as it hits your helmet when you lower your helmet as close and forward to the windscreen as possible. For anyone who doesn´t do it now....wear hearing protection with this bike!!  You´ll save your hearing over time.

text: I have a 1998 BMW 650 with 3000 miles.  I have had it for 1500 miles and I am a fairly new rider, especially when it comes to mechanical issues.  I have a couple of questions for anyone who might there...I just got the bike back from a repair shop and it seems that the oil is well over the max. on the stick.  This is the second time this has happened.  Do you think the shops mis-judge, or am I supposed to be running over the max on this bike?  Probably a stupid question but there it is.  Second, they put on what must be a very nice chain at $120, so...how do I take care of it?  I have read a number of cheat sheet but I am still confused....it seems that I wipe clean then apply a silicone lube spray...is that correct?  If so, how often? Third, what is the confort range for this bike.  I have gotten a number of different answers.  One person said keep it at 3100 rpms, but if I do that I won´t be able to go over 55 mph.  I think I have the ear for where to be in the powerband around town, but I recently went 300 miles on the highway at an average speed of 73 with the rpms around 5000.  Is this too high for steady driving?  I would greatly appreciate answers if you have them.  Thanks, Garth

author: Jorge (Spain,EU)
text: Hi Garth, I have the same bike and it is very easy to check the oil level. When the engine is cold, put the bike on its vertical support, start the engine and do not move anything. Just let the engine and oil pump work for 2 minutes. Then stop the engine and the level of oil is ready to be measured. It is very important not to drive with the oil lever over the maximum, nor under the minimum. To oil the chain I use Putoline specific Chain Oil, nothing special. Just avoid the chain to get dry. It also depends on the weather, or if you drive over dusty ways. Regarding the comfort range I do not know what to say. I do not care much about it with my bike. On the highway I use to go between 4.000 and 5.000 rpm. However here in Europe the speed limit is higher (about 75 Mph) so I do not have potencial problems with the road police. If you change the oil appropiately, and let the engine get warm before raising the rpm, you hava a cheap maintenance bike for a very long time. Regards, Jorge.

author: JCBII
text: I recently purchased my F650GS used from a local BMW dealer.  It had approx. 2000miles on it when I took it off the dealer´s lot.  Since then I have put 400miles on it, which have been primarily for commuting to/from work on surface streets.  I have, however, had the chance to take it out on the freeway a few times.  I thought about waiting a while before posting here, but figured I could always post a follow-up comment after a few more miles have been put on this bike (especially after I get the chance to take it on a few tours). Likes:  Looks - Obviously, I wouldn´t have purchased this bike if it didn´t look so nice.  The overall appearance is stunning, in my opinion.  Curves are in all the right places.  Power - For a dual purpose (Called "Enduro" in my days) bike, the power is surprising.  I´ve never ridden this type of bike at speeds around 80mph without any worries.  It really seems to come alive at around 4000rpm, which I consider to be the sweet spot for this motorcycle.  80mph will require 5000rpm, though, but doesn´t seem to bother the engine one bit.  I guess the dealer was right when he told me that this bike can be ridden cross country.  Fairing and Instrumentation - Fairing really works at keeping most of the wind off of me.  Some buffeting on my helmet, which I already had expected.  Instrumentation is easy to read, even with direct sunlight on it.  Gas cap - Love this!  Cool looking, and very functional location just under passenger portion of the seat.  Filling up was never so fun! Dislikes:  Primarily, the seat.  Damn thing slopes towards the tank (The fake one, of course) and has me sliding into it all the time.  I have adjusted to it for some reason and seem to be able to resist the forward slide a little more than when I first bought the BMW.  I´m considering replacing it with a Corbin seat, but I would really like to know if they have adequately addressed this problem before I shell out $300 for one.   Low-end power - Not much torque under 3000rpm.  This really makes lane-splitting a problem for me.  Cars get the jump on me until I can get the rpm up enough to catch up to, and pass them.  As a result, I have avoided lane splitting while commuting, and just hang back in my lane.  Winds at high speed - Although I would actually have to rate this bike favorably when considering that it is a dual-purpose bike, it can be significantly affected by winds when traveling at speeds greater than 60mph.  I guess that should be expected considering that it only weighs around 400lbs.  While in traffic on the highway, I was pushed about by wind turbulence from other cars.  Disconcerting at first, but I am getting used to it.  Also, crosswinds can push you from one side of the lane to another if you are not quick enough to react.  Let´s face it; it´s just not a heavy street cruiser (or even a medium weight one, for that matter).   No fuel gauge - There is an idiot light that comes on when you have a little over a gallon of gas left.  But for the price you pay for one of these machines, you would have expected an actual fuel gauge. Actual problems:  Sometimes, I am unable to pop the tranny into neutral when sitting at a light.  When this occurs, I just keep the clutch lever pulled in until I get the green light.  By the time I get to the next red light, I am able to put it in neutral.  For some reason, this just isn´t that big of deal to me. What could BMW do to improve the F650GS?  Well, for me, the bike would be about perfect if it had a fuel gauge, a better designed seat, and more low-end power.  Otherwise, this bike is a winner in my book. JCBII

text: Hi everyone. I have a new C1 125, and am now thinking of changing the engine to the 200cc. Has anyone any info as to what it takes and cost??

author: Frank Thier
text: Hi, the easyest way is to buy a new BMW C1- 200 crankshaft with 58,7 mm stroke (original 50 mm) and a BMW C1- 200 cylinder kit with 62 mm bore. Then you achieve the original 176,31 ccm of the C1- 200. Only changing to the BMW C1- 200 cylinder results in 150,95 ccm. I´m just thinking of buying a cylinder kit from Malossi with 67 mm bore, this results in 176,28 ccm without changing the crankshaft. But I have to check if the Malossi- cylinder fits with the BMW cylinder- head. The cylinder is for a Aprilia Leonardo 125 or Scarabeo. They have all the same Rotax- engine 120. Write an e-mail if you need parts. Best regards Frank

author: will
text: This is the real Achilles heel of what is basically a great idea.  This is The most expensive BMW motorbike to service by a long way.  In the initial warranty period I was paying for two services a year at the prescribed 4500 mile intervals.  These were in excess of £300 per service! that´s £600 per year, which would have doubled the price of the bike in five or six years and That excudes the two new tyres a year I have been averaging. A word of warning ragarding rear tyres.  Not everyone will do this for you as it can take anything up to an hour to remove and refit the wheel neccesitating a considerable amount of dismantling.  Wattling tyres did it for me for the cost of the tyre (about £50)whilst the dealership where I bought it wanted £150 on top of the tyre cost! I now do most of my own servicing which is time consuming rather than difficult as you have to take off most of the body panels to do some fairly minor jobs.  BMW do not make this easy for owners as they do not like to sell special tools and when you order one it can take weeks or months to arrive.

author: NN

author: ' heart of a lion '
text: Most people will never have ever ridden, or should I say ´driven´ a BMW C1, yet plenty will have laughed when one went by. Well laugh if you must but if you´ve never tried one then the laughs are on you, for missing out on one of life´s two-wheeled sensations. Not only are these two-wheeled flying machines nippy around town, they are also very capable long-haulers too! In a world thats daily becoming more restricted and dull, the C1 is a real blast of excitement, a veritable laugh all the way wether blowing-off ALL the traffic or cruising the motorways at a steady 65mph with a most welcome 70-plus MPG. Now who´s laughing ? Its a lifestyle, its a revolution, its something you cant wait to get at the controls of, its a sensation.(lasts as long as you like too !!!) Roadholding is terrific in the dry and a challenge in the wet. Acceleration is fantastic, fast enough to be ahead of everything from the lights, including motorbikes !´Where did you get to fella?´ The very design of the C1 means you are very well protected from the elements, the windscreen wiper and washer is a revelation and the bodywork fits around you for total reassurance. Double crossover seatbelts are the icing on the cake. Its like flying your own personal jet fighter ! So if you, like most people have´nt tried a C1 then believe me you´re missing out on one of the wonders of life still(only just) available to mankind.Best Wishes, johnnyjota.

author: will
text: It HAS got the heart of a lion - a Rotax engine. Unfortunately it is in the body of a wart hog and a very heavy one at that.  The standing start is great due to low gearing but on the move the 185Kg plus rider´s weight starts to show especially uphill.  The engine does a great job in the circs but is mismatched for the vehicle - and I have got the 178cc (aka 200). As for the 125cc, don´t even consider it.  I would recommend 300 or 400cc as a more appropriate sized lump. As it is though, the weight of the bike puts a tremendous strain on the drive train and tyres which all have a ridiculously short life (e.g. drive belt 4500m, rear tyre 5000m etc)

author: Hills
text: I have always used fully synthetic two stroke oil in my RS. The power valve was cleaned around 8-900 miles ago and it´s started clogging already – the aprilia dealer suggested it should last between 2-4000 miles before needing cleaning. I don´t ride it full throttle all the time but I´m not riding with it at 3 thousand all day. Every trip gets the revs up to 11 thousand at least once; do you have to ride it like an %&#!¤-hole to stop it clogging; could the power valve be faulty? Could riding it with a clogged power valve have damaged it in anyway? Took it on motorway for a couple of miles with the needle between 9 & 10 thousand but if anything it made it worse. At the moment it performs like my old yamaha tzr 50 but if you force it up to the power band (takes ages in present condition) it will force the valve open and it will perform almost like it would normally. The throttle is like an on/off switch with a long delay feature. The bike is rideable but I´m not riding it until it goes in for power valve cleaning tomorrow, trust me it will be 15 miles of hell with white van man able to out accelerate a 33 bhp (more like 5) rs125. If anyone has any suggestions I´d be glad to hear them. Thanks.

author: Kevin Cogge
text: If you want a more reliable moped you have to take the DNA (it´s a four stroke and those are more reliable than the two stroke). Aprilia rs 125 is a two stroke (so less reliable) but the top speed lies way beyond where the DNA will ever go. The newest rs 125 also has plastic wheels in the engine so if they break --) engine doesn´t get enough oil --) get´s stuck. It´s really a choice between reliable but slow (+- 60 mph) or fast (could get up to 100 mph) but not one of the most reliable bikes.

Never leave ur lights on when not riding or u will be bmp starting the bike :) Its a two stroke as u no so use the best oil u can get and keep it clean. Alwas let the bike warm up to at least 50 degrees its a performance bike it runs like shit hen its cold. This is the best 125 out there forget the rs Its a toy compared to the mito. Keep it well maintained and it will go for well at least 30,000k lol :)

author: NN
text: I´m going to buy a mito and I was wondering if there ar any commen problems and so on

author: NN
text: Hi, I own a 1991 Cagiva Mito, its the italian import, but is restricted for UK use. i picked it up second hand with 41,500KMS on the clock (circa 25,700 mile) so far i have experienced coolant problems (water pump seal failing, which therefore reqiured a gearbox flush out) the starter motor bearings and bushes "abused" and the valve on the front tyre is starting to show its age.. reasonably reliable, i couldnt tell you the MPG / OCR i havent rode it long enough, if u like loud nippy attention seekers this is your bike, personally though im kinda too old and too big for a mito, now (hey im 20 years old, 5´ 8" 14st (ish)) but the old mito is a different breed to the new (1996-on) having being styled differently and in 2000 recieving a new mill (6 spd instead of 7) but the newer mitos have been known to fail within the 1st 12k if not properly looked after, personally, i think a few extra bhp would seriously do the mito alot of good, its a good bike, bit over priced but hey thats the 125cc market thru and thru in the UK :)

author: Tuomas
text: Should I buy a Mito, RS, NSR or Varadero 125? Give your opinion  and some arguments. Thanks!

author: andy
text: nsr is ugly.. but cheaper... aprillia is nicest but expensive but does 112. mito jus looks like a cheap ducati but still nice. wot is the varadero??? who it made by?? woty bout the husgvarna SM 125 S? very nice...i love supermoto´s.... any one know teh speed of that??

author: adam
text: The vardadero is a honda 4 stroke, kinda, dakar racing style bike, not really in the same league performance wise compared to the other three mentined. The aprilia is a decent bike, good handling power, top spoeed etc, but its so unreliable it will cost you more in teh first year on new bits( not included on warranty) than it would to insure, my friend has hadone for 8 months from new and it blew up on him, needed full engine rebuild, new clutch cost 150 quid, plus speedo cables a fragile and break easily it has also been known to have flames in the exhaust. the mito is as quick as the rs, smoother acceleration ( not slower mind) ten times more reliable and will definately cost less to run. The nsr is a good all round bike, quicker acceleration than the itailian bikes, but not as powerful and does not top out as high, being honda its unliklely to break down, it is really the sort of layed back sports bike, than the extreme of the itailian. i would by the mito.

author: roger
text: i recently bought a 2000 k1200lt bmw-- i am a big man and have ridden over 200 different bikes in my 47 years of motorcycling.. i have owned at best 27 different motorcycles..i have to say -- the gl1800 and the k1200 lt are equal in performance in most aspects. both are equally difficult to service but i have to give the k1200lt the overall edge in handling and it is 100 lbs lighter.. the reason i bought the k bike is lower purchase price. the k bikes have always been horrible investments but always extremely reliable.. i have owned 5 k bikes since 1993 and never once been towed.. my friend owns a full service motorcycle repair center and he says the wing is an electrical nightmare..

author: Pontiac T/A 428
text: The Trike You see here will go 100 mph. Will not be blown around at that speed. Runs Straight and True But a little scary the first few times. Recommended cruise all day at 70-75 mph is safe.Put the Music on and be completely comfortable riding the best bike ever. Trike by Trike Kit is Reliable and comfortable Kit with Independent Rear Wheel drive.Dual Disc Brakes in the Rear and Dual Disc Brakes in the Front allows stable and fast stops.The 1500cc Motor is Known to go some 400,000 Thousand Miles before having to be rebuilt.The windshield blows the air over your head and the Engine Heat through the Vents works well in the winter. Use the side Vents to redirect the heat away in the summer. Best of both worlds. It´s like A/C at times.Twin Electric Fans Cool the Motor that is Liquid cooled as well as air cooled.Duel seats , rear being higher for the Passenger allows her to see everything and feel proud in turn with her arm rests and drink holders if you want them. A Cadillac is the only comparison to this Vehicle.Cruise above 40 mph with the Cruise Control and Brake or Gas out if you wish to.Five Forward speeds is like having an Overdrive. Reverse uses the starter Motor and requires a small amount of shifting your weight front and back to release the Electric Motor to Sync with the Reverse Handle , Using the Reverse Switch Simultaneously.Plenty of Carpeted storage in the Lower Trunk that has a latch release left over from the Gold wings original package,Three in all, optional place for additional Fuel Tank . One is used to lock the upper Trunk and one is not used at all. The Rear Air Shocks Compressor is now to fill the Tires with air, all the Attachments are Included. The Rear Leaf Springs are Car type Leaf with Independent Rear Dual Shafts Perpendicular, like the Corvettes.The

author: Tooneyman
text: Have noticed a slight noticeable vibration that I think is coming from  the rear tire or wheel of my Goldwing when leaning the bike to the left at an angle (any angle). Kind of like when you’re going down a highway off-ramp at 40 mph. It feels exactly like you’re riding on the edge of a knobby tire or riding on the edge of your tire on an uneven road made of bricks. I would say it’s similar to those grooves cut in the interstate highways near the shoulder that makes a big sound and you can easily feel when you run over them The vibration I feel on the bike is not near that extreme and doesn’t make a loud sound like those grooves do, but it’s similar.  It’s not a shimmy type of thing. It reminds me of a studded snow tire where you can feel each stud. Riding the bike up straight or leaning the bike to the right does not ever produce the vibration. One guy I talked to on a Goldwing forum online suggested it could be a chipped bearing, but I think that would be fairly easy to see when you spin the rear wheel and it would make a distinct noise, too. I checked the tire for any bulges or damage the other night but didn’t see anything. I disengaged the clutch and applied the rear brake while the problem was happening, but there was no change. The vibration was still there. The rear tire is a Metzler and is the correct size for the bike with a lot of tread left.  There is some slight cupping and uneven wear on the left side of the tire, but it’s pretty minimal. Nothing that would look like it would make a noticeable vibration you could easily feel. Not sure how old the tire is. Guess it could be old and dried out to where the tread on the left edge is pretty stiff now and when you’re riding on the tire edge you feel every inch of the tread design.  Just not sure. Any help would be appreciated.

I was in getting brake for my 2000 Valk when I heard some negotiations going on between an elder gentleman and the sales manager at the supply company I use. Next to them was the most beautiful candy red SE. Once they had a break in the conversation, I had already determined that the dealer would not pay what the man wanted mainly because he was not buying another bike. I took my phone out and checked the value on the net. I, being a vendor to the dealership, then offered a compromise. I offered to pay the man 700 less then he said he wanted and then said I would pay the dealership $200 for facilitating the deal. To my great and very happy surprise, they both said “I’ll take it”. Today, I must go home and clean out may garage to fit two bikes and all my tools that I use to do my own wrenching. I am counting the minutes until I pick it up. I am in Carlsbad, CA and ride about 150 miles a day, so this new (to me) GoldWing will do two thing for me, first, it will reduce the mileage build up and secondly, I will have wheel while my bike is at the chopper shop being painted and having some stuff installed (custom made) that will probably take the better of three months to do. WEEhoooo!!!!!!!!!!!

author: ENGLISHERIC
text: IF YOU ARE LOOKING AT BUYING A USA SPEC/IMPORT, I BELIEVE THAT THEY ARE NOT AS RESISTANT TO UK ROAD DIRT AS THOSE MADE FOR THE UK MARKET. MY OWN BIKE THAT WAS PURCHASED ORIGINALY IN THE USA BEARS THIS OUT AS IT LOOKS GOOD FROM THE OUTSIDE BUT REMOVE THE REAR WHEEL AND SEE THE AMOUNT OF RUST, IT IS SCARY, MY BIKE WAS NOT WASHED OFF TO REMOVE TRACES OF SALT OF THE ROADS IN WINTER PLUS YOU WOULD HAVE TO DISMANTLE THE BIKE TO GIVE IT A PROPER CLEAN, WHEELS ARE BARE ALOY AND NOT PROTECTED FROM CORROSIVE ACTION, SO BUY A UK IMPORT OR TAKE A GOOD LOOK AROUN THE BIKE, BETTER STILL GET AN EXPERT TO GIVE IT THE ONCE OVER

author: APM
text: Just purchased a 1988 Wing with 92,000 KM´s, had been sitting for a few weeks, there was quite a puff of smoke (blue) on start up which did clear after a minute or two! A Wing rider friend told me the earlier 4 cylinder modles did this as a result of oil collecting in the valve guides but the 1500 shouldn´t? Subsequent rideing has not shown any other smoking problems and the bike otherwise runs fine! Is it usual for this model to smoke like this on start up? If not what is the fix?

author: bcat
text: listen I have a 88 and it has 92 000   it smokes after not running for a few weeks and at initial start up for the season. This engine is likely to have some wear at this point. Will it affect performance not likely. It is just old and ride her like shes old, these are a low rpm engine so shift quickly. Look at the red line on a 1100 wing its like 9500 rpm you old wing redlines at 6500. Treat her nice and she will do another 60000 before you need service. Do be concerned about the carberator thats where your going to have a problem. Slide diaphrams are likely to fail..

author: bcat
text: Common for carb slides to fail near 100,000 Common for some smoke at start up after 80/90 ooo. Common for vacuum leaks to stimulate other problems such as cruze control issues. Common to run these bikes for 100-150000 without any major issues.

text: What is the difference between the Gold Wing and the Aspencade?  Also, are Aspencades still being made?

author: limitedslip
text: An Aspencade is one of the Goldwing Models.  I have a 1993 Goldwing Aspencade.  That year they also made the Goldwing Interstate (less options, no reverse gear system), and Gold SE (full loaded with everything).  Like a model of car, say Pontiac Grand Prix (LE SE GTP), they have different option packages basically.  Send me an email if you have further questions.

author: wing ding
text: i plan to buy a cream puff 1993 aspencade se 38,000 miles candy red adult owned what do you think the value is on this and how good was that year ad design thank you mark in nj

author: limitedslip
text: There weren´t any signifigant design changes for the 1520 cc goldwings (1988 through 2000), other than a drop in seat height and minor suspension upgrade in 1995.  So the design for the ´93s is an good as any in terms of reliability, which is extremely good.  My ´93 Aspencade just clocked over 136,000 miles last month and has never had any mechanical problems.  The performance is as good as new, it will still easily push 130MPH when I open it up, and doesn´t burn any oil.  That ´93 with only 38000 miles should be very reliable, sounds like a cherry pick.

This should be obvious as these are apples and oranges. However, I recently discovered the moto dollies for towing a moto and have decided I CAN haul something much heavier. The Sherpa is a great bike, but it just tires me  out (I am an urban rider) fighting the wind noise, etc.  So now, I am just looking for general info on a bike I enjoyed immensely in the year I had it. author: Peter
text: Hi I´m a 39 year old Ducati driver from Sweden. I bought a Ducati ST2 in 2003 and have had the opportunity to test the bike during three seasons. I hate to say it, bu my general opinion about the ST2´s reliability and perfomance is not very good. It looks good and is an excellent bike for touring on public highways and main roads but absolutely crap on narrow streets and in city traffic because of its sad performance on low revs. It is a must to replace the original front pinion with a smaller one to improve the driveablility in slow traffic. I also think it is heavy to manouver. I have had several problems with the bike. First the revolution counter stopped working, then valve springs on one cylinder broke. Later on oil started to leak when a gasket in the cylinder broke. That, together with a general feeling that I cannot rely on the bike and expensive maintenance costs made me sell it.

author: HDGoose
text: I was looking at the new ST4 in the dealership. The salesmans says the chain will last 30,000 miles is I lube and clean it every 500 miles. That mean on some trips i would have to clean the chain twice a day. Is the true? How many miles do you folks really see on the dive chains?

author: Rick
text: Hy Goose, I drive a 1997 ST2 with a small 14-teeth front sprocket for more torque. I´ve done app. 20.000 km with this chain so far and there is no real wear showing. I lube it every 300-500 km with grease from a spray-can shell or castroll). Greasing it (without cleaning) just before a longer stop or at the end of the day so the solvents can evaporate and solidify (otherwise the grease all ends up on the rim after 5 minutes). So I am quite satisfied so far, but I think 30.000 miles (48.000 km)is over-estimated. The chains and sprockets on Ducati are not very wide and oversised to keep the weight down(the chain and sprockets on my girl-friends Suzuki GS400E twin are wider and of a bigger diameter!!).  I would settle for 35.000 km maximum given frequent greasing. I understand your statement an I am looking into the possible alternativers of permanent greasing systems as the Scott Oiler(expensive and moderate succes). The German "CLS"-system looks promissing and well developed but has a prize that only is acceptable for professinal use. Regards, Rick

author: Bill Shepherd
text: 90,000 klm on my 2009 Honda CBF1000. Except for replacing stater and rectifier, its been perfect (Honda gave an extra 8 years warranty on those parts). From short 300 klm day rides to 4000 klm touring, it does it all in super comfort. I added 3 hard bags, tank bag, heated grips, automatic chain oiler, higher windshield,engine guard. Last tires were Michelin Pilot 5 which can take a heavy load, handle well and last longer. . Lots of engine torque so I can be in 6th gear and go 40 klm/hr then accelerate and not need to shift. The 1000cc makes insurance a bit high.Amazing gas mileage.

author: Tom R
text: Take the tank off. The two ignition coil connections for each side of the engine correspond to that side, but not the order. Connect the left most coil connection with the plug on the inside. Same convention with the other side of the engine. SO, if you were to arbitrary number from left to right the coil connection and the plugs it would be: 1 with 2, 2 with 1, 3 with 4 and 4 with 3

text: anybody can explain to me how the shifter works, never had a bike like this before!!!

author: tommy colang
text: The lower shifter is your main gear box 5 speed 1 down, 4 up, the upper shifter is your auxillary shifter,(like a low/high range split axle on a big truck.) how you decide to shift gears is totally up to you, there is no right or wrong way, you could leave the aux. shifter  down in low range riding at low speeds around town and shift to high range when you get out on open roads where your speed will be higher, or you can change on the fly whenever you decide for whatever torque range you are looking for. Hope this helps !!! Have fun !!!!

author: Dan
text: Hey guys, just a quick question, on my 94 cb1000, i find knicks in my gas tank that are apparently coming from my handlebars hitting when they are turned all the way to either side. I understand that the obvious solution would be to just not turn the bars all the way, but its a lot easier said than done. Just wondering if anyone has noticed this problem and found a solution. Thanks

author: CBrider
text: i know my bars get real close..but it looks like it isn´t the bars hitting on mine..look at the controls..the lines comeing off the clutch lever might be what is actuly hitting..you can loosen the control assembly and give it a little twist for clearance then retighten. should not take a lot of adjustment.

author: CB Phil
text: The only problem with trying to twist the controls is, there is a locator pin on the controls. you would need to enlarge this hole, or file down the pin (the controls can move about if this is done). you could on the other hand simple change the bars.

author: GradyDiver
text: I had a similar issue after bringing minne to a shop.  At one point when filling a very empty gas tank, I realized that teh tank had a lot of play.  I discovered that the front guides on the inside under teh tank were sitting on top of the rubber mounts.  I had to remove the rear gas tank bolt, slide teh tank back (very easy to do when empty) and place it on teh mounts correctly.  I bolted it back up, filled and drove away.  It took less than 5 minutes right at teh pump with a wrenh from the toolkit. Good luck.

author: Andy
text: Totally agree and would much prefer an H4 bulb setup with two light on for each of dip and full beam, looks better as well as better to see with (and be seen).  I believe that the CBF1000 alternator output is 330w max so this would need to be increased to run dual filament bulbs (A Honda cost cutting exercise ?, don´t know but anyway we shouldn´t complain too much given the amazing value of the bike in the first place). The standard light output is ok but it can be significantly improved.  Either a better quality 55w bulb such as Phillips xtreme power or Vision plus or higher wattage ´rally´ type bulbs. The Phillips xtreme power bulbs (or the Osram equivalent) really are much brighter than standard offerings and offer an excellent beam pattern.  Phillips and Osram manufacture to very high tolerances and much of the improvement is a result of the filament allignment within the bulb and hence also in relation to the reflector which in turn puts the light where you need it on the road (not ramdomly dispersing the light). Higher wattage bulbs (80w instead of the standard 55w)sound good and indeed will be physically brighter but there are real potential downsides.  Whilst the alternator will probably supply sufficient output to power the higher wattage bulbs just wacking them in will not necessarily solve your issues. First they run considerably hotter. Second the additional current still has to travel down the existing wiring loom and whilst I have not yet checked the wiring capacity it may need to be upgraded to heavier duty stuff.  Generally the thicker the wiring the lower the resistance which = more current reaching the bulb.  Then there are the weak points in wiring loom being any connection in the wiring particularly the spade connection on the bulb where even a marginally loose connection will lead to a resistance and loss of output at the bulb.  Does this make a difference to light output, too damn right it does. Third, the CBF 1000 has a 20w fuse protecting the lighting circuit and whilst this should be sufficient for the higher wattage bulbs I would certainly carry a spare fuse with me. Fourth, as far as I can tell they are not road legal in the UK and Europe but then, are we really bovvered? Fifth, importantly if anything did go aray with the wiring/charging circuit, always assuming the Honda techies notice, using the higher wattage bulbs would almost certainly invalidate the warranty. Again both Phillips and Osram make very high quality ´rally´ bulbs i.e. 80w H7.  I keep banging on about Osram and Phillips but like Mr Kipling making exceedingly good cakes Phillips and Osram make extremely good bulbs and all bulbs are most definately not equal. I am not saying that other manufacturers offerings are no good but from my personal experience of others particularly Ring and the Halfords brands, which whilst certainly bright gave an relatively indecisive beam structure and hence loose much of the bulbs potential (they seemed to spread the light everywhere and even the light pattern varied bulb to bulb as well!) Lastly get the lights allignment set up right.  My Honda dealer has had two goes on their beam setter but failed to get it right leaving high beam too high and dip marginally low resulting in a dark band between high and dip beam when on full beam - really annoying that.  The easiest way to deal with this I recon is to set the final height adjustment yourself in live conditions on the road. Bye the way I suggest you forget the ´ blue´ bulbs, they might look cool but blue light does not illuminate wet roads as well as white light. So there you have it.  My choice was the Phillips X-treme 55w bulbs, no wiring, warranty issues or other downsides and are a HUGE improvement over the original and very much worth the £34 cost. Andy

author: macbasham

author: geoffreys
text: G´day Brad, My hands go numb on pretty much any bike I´m riding. But in-line fours are definitely the worst with those high frequency buzzes. I speak with some experience, having a 97 CBR1000F and an 84 VF1000R in the garage and recently selling my Kawasaki VN1500. I tend to tense up as I ride with a constant throttle opening. Your hands are in the same position, holding the throttle at the same opening and, as a consequence, your shoulders and neck tense up as well. First my right hand numbs up and followed shortly after by my left hand. I bet you take your hand off the bars momentarily and give it a shake to restore feeling... My solution is to relax my upper body and let the blood flow to my hands again...simple as that! You feel that same cool rush and tingle as when you give your hands a shake. I´ve experimented on many rides and this really does do the trick...relax! 1100klms in 11 hours on the VF1000R from Newcastle to Melbourne last week proved it again, with only 3 stops for fuel on the way and no cruise control or palm rest on the throttle. Let me know how you go. Cheers, Geoff S.

author: Spanky
text: I think that question is hard to answer. I started out on a Honda Big1 (96) , and I have loved it. But then....I am 6 foot four and 17 stone. If you are a little person , go for something smaller and lighter...this is a proper heavy Muscle Bike ! If you are a big Person...Ideal! Reliable, fast ( a non too shabby 140mph), smooth and forgiving ride , good for touring too ( tho being naked....you get buffeted a bit with speed, so a good choice of helmet to eliminate some of the wind resistance is important. Still hellishly heavy to pick up if you fall  over tho !

Its an easy bike to ride one your used to the weight, im 5´9 and 65kg´s and now i have no trouble. My last bike was an R6 which looks great and geos very well, but the cb is much easier to ride and is so much more comfortable. You obviously dont get as many looks and at 20 years of age, that counts. But the cb is a great bike if you dont care about the image, my next bike will be a cb600, newer and lighter.

author: Dave Krier
text: I have owned this bike since the spring of 1999. It has incredible acceleration and power. I have taken a 400 mile one-way trip with little discomfort. The engine is extremely smooth. The transmission shifts very positively. 1996 was the last year for the 1000 CBR; this bike still gathers onlookers. The depreciation for this bike is very low; it holds its value well. This is a very satisfying bike to ride unless you travel long distances regularly. I get 50 mpg on the highway which is fantastic. The 5.8 gallon tank will go 290 miles before running dry.

author: pooky
text: go to www.chromeheads.org and find all your answers. The OEM quick release is made of plastic and prone to cracking and failing over time.  Replace with metal version from CPC.  Aftermarket from CPC directly or McMaster Carr or other supplier.  Much cheaper than going to BMW.  I replaced both of mine for less than $50 and that included shipping.  Good luck!

author: DOT
text: I have had one for a couple of years and 9,000 miles on nothing mechanically untoward has happened. In basic maintenance etc. you`ll need a special tool to get the oil filter off, chain tension is very easy. Have to take the tank off to change the plugs. If you are into DIY in a big way, then there is an official Kawasaki Service Manual. I had to go to the States for an English language version. It is Part No.99924-1245-05. Keep the rubber on the road. DOT.

author: Doug
text: After 27 years of not riding, I bought my second new Kawasaki bike- a new 2001 W-650. This was in May of 2003 as Kawasaki discontinued sales of the W-650 in the USA due to poor sales...it seems everyone wants a cruiser or crotch rocket these days...WELL NOT EVERYONE! This is a perfect standard motorcycle with classic 60´s Triumph looks and great 2001 Japanese quality! Plenty of power and a wide torque band. Powerful headlight too. Electric/kick start and a center stand! Bike even has a Tachometer! Worth every penny of the $6500 I paid for it!

author: Tom Pequeno
text: This is very easy bike to ride and handle in many ways. Low speed handling in traffic is excellent and it is also at home on the interstate. It is a perfect bike for the beginner to learn on and to keep or also just as much a blast for the verteran rider. It has a fine power to weight ratio along with shifting, clutch usage and it is reliable. The only faults I have experienced with the bike is that the front brake squeals and the fork springs are a bit too soft.  Both of those faults can be taken care of. Younger folks should not be put off by the retro style because this is a totally fun bike to ride and I highly recommend it. This is from a guy who has a Harley and a New Triumph Bonneville.

author: gwayne281

author: gwayne281
