author: NN
text: Come on Kawasaki, let´s know if there is a further model for 2003.

author: ichadwick
text: A nice, fun bike for general riding. Looks like the old Triumph Bonneville, but it´s a modern machine that handles much better. Light and easiy to steer, the W is a great around-town bike. Top speed is about 170 kmph so it´s fine on the highway, too. And it´s very beautiful - like beloved restoration. It has a centrestand and kickstart, too! Good for all levels of rider and a real head-turned.

author: Lou Mueller
text: Having passed retirement age, it´s fitting that I should have just bought a fully modern motorcycle that looks as though it was built 40 years ago.  The Kawasaki is not only a stunning imitation of the classic Triumph Bonneville, it is a superb, modern machine, with the sophistication one expects from today´s bikes: electronic speedometer/odometer with clock, electronic tachometer, front disk brake, overhead-cam engine, electronic ignition, and electric starter, plus a kick starter to boot, for old-times sake!  Like the distinguished cars of the 1930´s, the W650 has a classic gracefulness that endured in motorcycles for nearly 50 years because it was both beautiful and exquisitely functional. The handling on this bike is superb!  It is one of the best machines I have ever driven, very nearly equal to the BMW R60/5 I owned 25 years ago.  It rolls into the turns without becoming top heavy, and rolls back out nimbly and with no hesitation with a touch of power.  Because of its relatively modest weight [about 460 pounds (209 kG), wet], it´s easy to handle in town, yet it´s heavy and stable enough, and fast enough, to be an extremely pleasant tourer. This motorcycle is truly a machine that lets you know immediately that it is at your command, and completely under your control.  Thus far, it has never displayed any bad manners. Try one.  You should be very pleasantly surprised and delighted.

author: Vijay G Nattar
text: Hi friends, I am Vijaykumar G Nattar from Ahmedabad. I have hero honda cbz star model july 2005. According to my experience, i found that this bike is not as good as i expected to be. One day i rode my friend´s silver colour pulsar 150 dtsi disk brake type and found that this bike had amazing pick up and speed than the cbz. Also another problem i face with cbz is that you have to continously shift gears to 4th to 3rd to 2nd in low traffic and the bike does not gain normal pick up in 3rd or 4th gear in little traffic like honda unicorn or pulsar. In honda unicorn or pulsar, the bike can run at speed of 25 or 35 in 5th gear, so  even if the bike is in 4th gear u dont need to come down to 3rd gear or 2nd gear in medium traffic. But in cbz u compulsarily need to shift to gear 2nd other wise the bike will face knocking problem if u ride at lower speeds in 4th or fifth gear.

author: missionguzzi
text: Fuel capacity is 5 gal (plus or minus a tenth); stock bike will get 35-37 mpg under moderately quick riding conditions.  Power Commander III can help improve mileage, and/or performance, depending upon how you tune it.  Seat is fairly good, not causing any particular soreness after several hundred miles.  Depending upon your pants or your seating position, it can tend to pull your gonads.

author: John
text: Standard exhaust was practically silent at low and midrange revs. Do 100mph with no noise to attract the fuzz. Beat the much more highly tuned Kawasaki zzr1100 from a standing start drag race to 120mph. Could quietly and smoothly pull away from traffic lights at just over tickover 1100RPM and still beat all the tin-tops. 1430mm wheelbase and 105mm trail, sport bike chassis dimensions made it nimble in corners. gsf1200s top fairing allowed long long distance cruising without tiring. Punched well above it´s weight and was CHEAP to buy. Seat was only downside. Got crushed nuts if carrying a pillion.

author: Jda
text: So I had an issue with starting my 2001 bandit 1200s, I opened the casing to the gears and had oil in it(assuming it is to lubricate those gears).  The problem was that the large gear´s bolt became lose. I tightened back down and starter  worked again.  But in my first ride it started fine but after my 4th start I tried to go from neutral to first and the bike died. And the oil light came on. So I tried again and bike died again. Push started and got the bike home. Got the casing off and bolt was still tight, placed casing back on. Stuck on center stand and started bike with electric start and put into first gear fine(without dying). 1.) should oil be in those gears? (Probably stupid question) 2.) why would the bike die when going into 1st gear from neutral? Any help would be greatly appreciated, I´m just getting familiar with this bike! Thanks, Jason

author: Joe
text: I am having trouble starting my 1999 1200S Bandit. When I turn the ignition, I get power but when I press the starter, I get a click from under the seat and everything dies, no lights, no horn, or anything. I jostle the battery, get electricity back and try again. This goes on for a while and usually by about the fifth try, the starter engages and starts and the bike runs very easily without stalling. Continutity is fine from the battery to the starter. I am told it might be similar to an old Chrysler and their ceramic ballast resistors but I see no such animal in the circuit or in the Clymer manual. It is a new and fully charged battery. Thanks in advance for any help.

His e-mail address is iaincscott@yahoo.co.uk for any replies. author: slavy
text: I recently tried to use 02 GSF1200 carbs  in my GSX1100G and had very simmilar problem. I eventually went back to the original carbs, but I noticed that the air suction orfice of the Bandit is significantly smaller, compared to GSX1100G. While playing , i closed partually the air intake orfice of the GSX1100G and the problem almost disapeared. My feeling is that You will have to go at least 1 notch richer on the needle and probably 2 sizes up on the main jets. My Bandit is ´98 with 2 K&N pod filters and Yosh RS3 pipe. I have a jet kit, but it was installed by the previous owner and I just rejetted it to my liking. The bike has increadible performance, BUT the gas mileage sucks. I am getting 33 mpg,/7.5-8 l/100km/

author: Phillip
text: Hello, Well, I´ve owned a ´98 Bandit 12 S for over two years now and I can tell you that it is a tremendously fun bike...however, it is a beast and may be a bit much for a first time street bike...literally it is possible to bring the front up at will in either of the first two gears with no clutch work...all power wheelie.  A true torque monster.  Like I said, fun, but strong.  If you´re confident with the power, it it can be a very rewarding bike.  Fast enough to keep up with the sport bikes, comfortable enough to do trips. 3000 Canadian is an alright deal for the bike, but only if it´s been treated right.  The engine has been around so long it´s been tweaked so it´s damn near unbreakable.  However, 50,000 km is quite a bit of riding, but not a whole lot.  I´ve heard of people with over 100,000 miles on their bikes and their still truckin.  So just be sure the seller is on the up and up with all the maintenance...valve clearances especially.  About the exhaust, just make sure he´s jetted the carbs properly.  Aftermarket suspension work is also very popular for this bike, so ask if he´s put on new springs front or rear.  Lastly, do try to have him let you test ride it.  I´ve been screwed over before...used bikes are an iffy proposition without a test ride. That all said, I love mine to death.  Tremendous aftermarket potential with upgrades and power to boot.  Just be careful. Best of luck with the purchase...cheers.

text: New to the Bandit, I am.  About 3 months.  Very pleased, overall.  Interesting that this site gives 139 as top speed- I´ve seen 135 on mine once, and that was fast enough for me!  Didn´t know I was almost at terminal velocity on it. Only complaints I have so far: 1) clutch is not up to the torque engine puts out, slips at high speed when I pass without downshifting- engine has the torque for this, clutch should be able to keep up. 2) The Bandit is more "sports," less "touring" than I would like.  Reason I am on this site is to find out what Suzuki´s bike that is about same size, but more touring, less sport, is.  Anybody know? email me. thanks.

author: Andrew
text: I have a 1999 Black Bandit S (deep metalflake blue, actually). With about 7000 miles on it, it is just getting broken in. One can always add aftermarket improvements (I will, soon), but this is one of the nicest packages I´ve seen out there, stock. Comfortable seating, adequate suspension for one-up, subtle brutish handsomeness, and more power than you will usually be able to use (but with LOTS of serious aftermarket horsepower potential). This bike is a winner, destined to be an enduring icon of Suzuki brilliance well into the future. This winter, I am going stage 2, and giving the beast a couple of cosmetic touches. That´ll do for a year or two. Models from 1998-2000 have the square headlight (not the Japanese cartoon-like cross-eyed dual beam thing of 2001 and forward). Find one of these with low miles and start negotiating. You´ll thank me.

author: speedfreak
text: i also own a 1999 gsf 1200s bandit in british racing green, commonly mistaken for jap racing green, with a stage 3 kit, yosh rs3 exaust, fender eliminator kit, flush mount markers and i am in the process of buying a headlight conversion that will turn the original headlight in to dual round headlights, (one low beam both high.)

I use it for commuting and fun. Yesterday it kept saying to me ´go faster, faster´ but I said ´no, no, you´re not run in yet !´....

text: 140 miles to the reserve is about right.

text: About 100 in San Francisco just driving in town only. Then it´s a good idea to get more gas.  I got a 2003 GSF1200S.  Mucho power tho.

author: 01 Rider
text: Compare to what?  A car?  Yes, most cars can go over 200 miles in one tank but your Bandit only has around 5 galons.  At 40MPG that is 200 miles to dry (ie pushing it to a gas station).  The reserve is over 1 galon so that would be around 140-150 when reserve goes on.  Check out fuelly.com to compare it to other owners (better yet join them and add your stats to the rest). Remember 100hp for something this light (including rider) is a LOT of power per pound.  These things give mileage at par of the best of the econo car but perform at par to the best of sports cars and you are complaining?

author: jollygreen
text: i must say i am sorry but i do not know too much about motorcycles. my first was a honda ascot.  i rode a katana, and an sv 650, but now i own a gsf 1200s. i was wondering, is it possible to put a 6th gear in the transmission, or cannabalize another suzuki bike for a transmission with six gears.  if i can, would it actually better the bikes overally performance, or will it jsut be a waste of time and money??  if you don´t mind, please email me if anybody out there knows or cares. thanks

author: Eugene
text: The bike does not need a sixth gear but if you want more out of it try a tooth less on the rear sprocket this will increase acceleration and it is unlikely that you will loose anything on top speed.

author: Remsky
text: If you go smaller on the back you will loose accelaration and gain top speed.Smaller on the front will give you more take off and higher RPM in top gear(harder on gas).

author: NN
text: I do not think you can install a 6 th gear,if you could your ratios would be off,what I mean by this is that the gears have a ratio to climb a scale evenly somewhat,if you add a 6 th gear the ratios will be uneven.It is like dividing 1 by 6,or 1 by 5,there is quite a difference.This bad boy is barely working in fifth gear,just have fun.I have a 2002 bandit 1200 .If you want to bring your RPM down just install a smaller sprocket(3 teeth less) on the back or one tooth bigger on the front.A very cheap way compared to insatlling a 6 th gear.

text: WELL, I HAVE JUST BEEN NOTIFIED VIA MY E-MAIL THAT A DOUBL-BUBBLE WINDSCREEN IS ALREADY AVAILABLE, UNFORTUNATELY IT IS BACK ORDERED IN MANY STATES. NOW MY NEXT MISSION HOP-UPS. DON´T GET ME WRONG I AM NOT GOING TO REBUILD A BRAND NEW BIKE. MY INTENTIONS CURRENTLY ARE ONLY A SLIP-ON AND A FEW DRESS PIECES. author: NN
text: I have a 2001 1200 bandit, at first I thought it needed a 6th gear, but if you get the chance to notice other bikes and what revs they sit on at around 120km/h, the bandit dosen´t realy need 6th. The wind protection is good but you may find that it doesn´t seem that way cause it has a gap at the very front which reduces the waffle effect other bikes get, so it may feel like the wind is always on you but it is better than the waffle effect

My Harley is a monster to hold in line at any speed but, the Bandit feels glued to the road.

author: highrev1
text: Hello Tbyrd.. I also have an o2 b1200s that I bought a couple weeks ago.  This bike is awsome looking, great power-especially in low to midrange and pretty decent handling for the weight of the bike. I have a yoshimura rs-3 exhaust and it sounds awsome. If you are looking for an exhaust at this point  yoshi is good, or look to www.holeshot.com  for great aftermarket upgrades for your bike. Also check out www.maximum-suzuki.com  It is a great forum site with a lot of seasoned bandit owners. You are right, the bike could use a little more wind protection and there is aftermarket ones available.  A 6th gear would be good also, but you might have a hard time finding that on the aftermarket :) Awsome bike.. wheelies like a mo fo.. have fun man !!

author: Marcio Teixeira
text: i have a KTM lc4 640 Supermoto for +/- 1 year, i got it with +/- 30.000 km and the only thing had to change was some litle selants on the water pump shaft, nothing special.. Handling and Performance : - Verry fun to drive in city ,roads with slot twists and turns, breaks realy fast and accelarates evern Faster! Verry Powerfull engine that not every rider can explore the full potencial of all that power. top Speed of mine now its arownd 160 Km/h still goes like crazy up hill. Cons: Engine Vibrations, small fuel deposit 9,5L does around 130Km then hits Reserve that does about 35-40Km +/- Seat is Sporty so not too confortable but its normal in these types of bikes. OVERALL : if you like Supermotos and dont want to be all the time wasting monny in maintanance this is the bike for you.. Its Carburator engine But if you ever need to adjust anything its far better than the injection that are verry expensive to repair . if you have anny questions fell free to ask

I don´t know why this would be, but there are two low speed crashes on utube and I had one as well (at about 10kph) All in all, the bike is beautiful, reliable (it is my only transport so it is used everyday)pretty economical, but expensive. I bought it brand new so I can´t help with re sale prices but all aprilia stuff is expensive.

author: L.D. Blake
text: I cant remember if it is 50cc and under, or 49cc and under to be classified as a moped by the dmv. But I have heard of people licensing their pocket bikes (47cc) as mopeds just to be legal on the road. The RS 50 might not be a true 50cc because they usually round up the displacement. Like the GSXR600 is actually 599cc. If they are classified as a moped by the dmv that would be allot cooler than a regular nerdy moped (I laugh when ever I see someone on a moped especially grown men). An alternative to the higher priced Aprilia RS 50 would be the Yamaha YSR 50. I’m not an expert on the matter but it’s defiantly something to research.

author: gachot
text: the 50cc Aprilia class, derbi, or any yamaha 50cc (essential the TZR) all classified as mopeds as far as I know. since all of them have an engine size under 50cc, (all about 49,7cc...). yeah, there you go.

author: Fahd
text: I have an Aprilia RS50, and techincally they are mopeds because they have a 50cc engine meaning 16 year olds can ride them, but once you get on the Aprilia RS50 and start riding, you soon realise this bike is far from a ´moped´ as every aspect of it other than the engine is very similar to its 100cc older brother (the Aprilia RsV 1000).

author: lukerobs
text: No its not legal to ride any sort of motorbike intill you are 16 years of age, at the age of 16 you must then complete a CBT course which can burn your pocket at around £100+ after completing the course which lasts almost 5 hours you can only then ride a 50cc MOPED not motorcycle (MOPED = 2 wheels under 12.5 BHP max speed of 30MPH) you will also need TAX, MOT and Insurance which doesnt come cheep for a Race Replica, you also need a british standard helmet and 2 "L" plates 1 MUST be at the front and another MUST be at the back. All in all after all this its quite fun if you de-restric the MOPED therefore making it a motorcycle, (not legal till your 17).

author: Adam
text: Hi, i i am a 16 year old and passed the CBT on friday 9th just 2 days ago. Tonight saturday 10th i finished work and thought i´d go for a little ride this is about hlf 11pm so it was quite cold but not majorly cold so it shouldnt have affected te bike. It did start but didnt stay on so i had to keep revving and then pull away and keep moving. whenever it was in any lowish revs it sounded like it was going to cutout so i had to keep them high after getting a little way down the main road it started picking up and sounding a bit better so i thought well ill give it a little ride maybye it just needs warming up or something but after about 5 mins it just suddenly started losing power gradually slowing and slowing til it stopped and cut out. When it cut out it made this strange power losing sound if you know what i mean and that was it i was stuck if ever i managed to get the snjine on againas soon as i gave it some throttle the bike lost power again Eventualy i gave up and started paddling home on the bike and i was managing to paddle it wuite quickly so then i started it and it worked and i drove home with the revs really high without stopping, as soon as i got home when i went down the gears to go up onto the driveway it lost power again this was tonight and here i am now woried to death that i might have a big problem on my hands. it could be a small ting but it could be a big thing i know nothing about these things at all and really need some advice coz im not going to be able to sleep tonight !!!

author: DaRenegadeRacer
text: Hi, The top-speed of the 2004 RS 50 is different from country to country. I´m from switzerland, and in our country we´ve got the original Cylindre, original exhaust-system, etc. form italy!!(the italian cylindre etc. is the same like the switzerland-cylindre...) So, my RS 50 2004 runns a bit faster as 100km/h!! Thats realy cooool!!! In other countries like germany, they have special versions of engines!! Ist very bad for them riders, ´cause they have less then the half of power as its normal from italy-versions!! The RS 50 from Aprilia is a very good bike, all most perfekt!! It´s areodynamic line is PERFEKT!! the Rs 50 not only has the irresistible "look" of a Grand Prix bike. Its exceptional aerodynamic line has been prepared in the wind tunnel, while its technology achieves a level never seen before in this displacement. The Aprilia RS 50 is one of the best, if not the best, bike in it´s class!!! FOR REAL!!! MFG da renegade racer!:D

author: Mr RS..
text: The Aprilia RS50 Max speed is 65mph. (Im 135pounds). It takes a long distance to reach this speed!! You will shift 3to4 times to get out of an intersection.. Its about 10mph per Gear.. Lower in the first 2Gears.. Will the Bike go UPHills 50mph.. It Cant, 30 to 40Mph It can, It also Depends on the Degree of the Hill..  Anyone Who Says the Apilia RS50 Can 90Mph Stock is telling lies!! You will find out when you Buy it.. With a 75cc Kit & carb, it will go 80Mph.. But That is Maxed out on the Rpms, This is with a Aero Race pipe, You Can gain a Little MPH by Changing the Sprockets..  Its a Nice Bike.. I Does not have Power!! 5.32HP @ The wheels.. 75cc Kit will push It Up to 10HP..  The Most I have Seen on a "REAL" Dyno was 14hp. & The bikes total $$ to Get it to that,about $3000. to get it to that level..  Good Luck!!  Test Before you Buy..  Or  Go to Apilia.com  & e-mail them, they can give Good info!! I have Been Riding the RS50 FOR 2yrs. Im Still hoping the RS250 Gets Fuel injection, This Could help it, make it to the USA Streets!!

text: Hi, I own a Bajaj Discover 125 CC DTS-i. I bought it in the year 2006 Feb2. The bike has clocked 35000+kms. This usually happens when i have rode the bike for around 15-20 mins and stopped for around 10 mins and again start my journey. Evey time this problem arises, I would have to push the choke and raise the accelerator too much, Only then the bike springs back to life. I visited the local Bajaj showroom for checkup, they said it may be due to some Carburettor problem and they cleaned the same in ´Acetone´ and checked the electrical connections also. There was nothing wrong with my electrical connections. Also I have fitted a new battery. There is NO problem during cold start also. The bike starts off good and runs well. But the problem occurs when I am crossing a Speed Breaker or going really slowly in 1st or 2nd gear. Am really worried about my bike. Please help me out..!

author: Ewade
text: I traded my 2016 Harley Heritage (Twin Cam 103 with Stage 1 upgrade and Vance & Hines slipons, and new Metzler Cruisetec tires) for a 2021 Road King and the difference is major.  I was at my Harley dealership on a Sunday for an event and saw the Road King - I had the salesperson setup the bike with a tallboy seat and it made a big difference.  I am 6ft tall and 250 lbs, the Road King is about 150lbs heavier than my 2016 Heritage.  The weight difference of the Road King was not a major concern, but the bike was so much better in all aspects - especially the suspension and brakes.  The only thing I need to upgrade is the handlebars (14in apes)/grips, LED lights all around and a Stage 1 upgrade with some Vance & Hines Tourquer 450 slipons (I had the dealer install the heel/toe shifter).  A passenger backrest and rear seat rack is also a possible upgrade for me.  The Milwaukee 8 engine is a noticeable upgrade and the bike drops to 850rpm on idle smoothly when the engine gets hot.  The engine heat is not an issue like it is on the 2016 Heritage 103 Twin Cam which caused quite a few leg burns.  The Road King suspension performance on the highway is totally 100% superior at 60mph and above - bike rides smooth and stable at all speeds on local streets.  Two up riding is also much more comfortable and stable on highway and local streets.  No problems braking on the Road King on uneven or any type of bad road conditions.  This is crazy, but after purchasing the Road King I discovered it is also much better at doing wheelies.  I could not be happier with my decision to upgrade to the 2021 Road King.

author: robert haines
text: My 2002 Road King was shutting down when I went to pass. It would pop thru the carb and have no power. I checked everything I could think of. I thought about it one night and knew when popping out a carb means lean fuel mixture. I started checking the fuel line and noticed the petcock on the tank had a vacuume shutoff on it. So I removed it and installed a petcock without the shutoff and plugged the vacuume hose and now it runs fine. Full RPM when passing now.

author: robert haines
text: 2002 FLHR cam chain tensioner. My Road King had 16000k on it so I checked the cam chain tensioners. They were over half worn and I got worried of engine failure. So I installed S&S 509g cams, lifters, and pushrods and eliminated the chains and it runs so much better.More low end torque and power.

When I got to Asheville I had full lever resistance and my front brake was working 100 percent. No problem for last 600 miles. ??? Did I get fluid to hot? When it cools it starts to work.

Opinions?

author: WFox
text: Had oil Temp gauge installed and then an Oil Cooler and HD synthetic oil after the first service. Now have 16,000k on it and the rear cylinder has never needed to shut off, even on hot days, (95F) on short trips through the city. The Temperature dropped 55*F from 285F to 230F on hot days. Iv herd synthetic is good up to 300F so because the temperature was getting close to that during break in and the rear cylinder was shutting off a lot in traffic as well, I decided there and then to switch from the regular HD Motor Oil. Also put on a Center stand which is a new thing. Some fun putting it on, but it works like a hot damn. Just needs a short extension pipe for a better reach but its all quite well hidden, looks ok and hasnt draged so far. I just wish there was more than 3 3/8 inch screws holding it on as its an extra 12 pounds. The seat could be a little better,..has anyone tried a Mustang seat or something comparable? Could use some info on Tire choices as mine didnt get included in the recall so I´ll be buying. Red/Gold 09 FLHRC

He went on to say that the stock bike comes with a poly???? buffer in the handlebar attach point that is intended to dampen road & engine vibration.

author: RMC
text: mine were actually loose as the easily moved up and down with little effort.  The HD mechanic tigthtened them and they no longer move.  He also told me about the poly bushings and said you can install rigid aftermarket bushings.  Only problem is they don´t absorb much vibration.  Hope this helps....

text: I HAVE OWNED THIS BIKE FOR A LITTLE OVER A YEAR AND RIDDEN IT ABOUT 3500 MILES. IT HAS 34000 ON THE MOTORCYCLE. ENGINE IS SMOOTH AS CAN BE UNTIL ABOVE 2300 RPM OR SO AND IT SOUNDS LIKE TOP END NOISE SIMILIAR TO THE OLDER PANS AND SHOVEL ENGINES. DOESNT SOUND LIKE CAM CHAIN TENSIONERS. MORE OF A HARD TAPPING LIKE POSSIBLE A LIFTER LEAKING DOWN AT HIGHER RPMS . HAS ANYONE HAD THIS PROBLEM. OTHER RIDERS TELL ME ITS NORMAL ON THE TWIN CAM ENGINES, OTHERS SAY NOT NORMAL AT ALL. I HAVE HAD OVER 20 HARLEYS IN MY DAYS , BUT THIS IS THE FIRST TWIN CAM.  I LOVE THE BIKE!  JUST CONCERNED ABOUT THE NOISE.

text: The other swithe on my bike is the cruise control

author: RC
text: ok so now how do you use the cruise control, because I have the same thing and dont know how to use thew cruse control

author: Stevo
text: You´ll notice a switch on the left side of ur handlebar underneath the turn signal..turn the switch on and a little red indicator light wiil appear in your speedometer..an identical switch is located under the right turn signal..when u reach ur desired speed..move that switch to the right and the red indicator light will turn green..u are now in cruise control. Deactivation is achieved by applying brakes, clutch or turning the left switch back off.

author: Kansas Slim
text: In answer to ypour original question, the toggle switch on the left switches power for any accessories you might add to the acessory plug under the seat.  Your headlight should always be on.  If it´s not on, its burned out or there is some other problem.

author: JTN
text: I recently tranded in my ´05 Wide Glide for an ´07 Road King Classic, and I absolutely love it.  No factory related problems at all...but a bit of a problem when I took delivery, a consequence of adding new V&H header pipes and mufflers prior to drive-out.  It seems that V&H didn´t do a very good job of testing the new 96 engine with their exhaust systems, and as a result their exhaust mappings were not up to date.  I rode the new bike for a day, and after nearly getting run over a couple of times (when I rolled on the throttle trying to overtake someone, and the damn thing died on me), I took it back to the dealer.  After 3 days of to´ing and fro´ing between V&H and The Motor Company, they finally got an exhaust map that would work.  Fortunately, the dealer did the right thing and put me on a demo bike, so I wasn´t without a ride during this time.  All in all, I love it.  Plenty of power with the new exhaust system and Stage 1; lots of bling, and a very comfortable ride.  I haven´t seen the need to replace the bars like lots of folks have, but I´m a tad over 6´ and I have long arms, so I don´t feel as if I´m reaching.  Not a single problem related to over-heating, and the clutch whine in 5th gear is barely noticeable.  I typically try to get into 6th gear as soon as I can...it just rides too smoothly there not to. ADD-ONs: V&H Dresser Duals, Stage 1 kit, Sundowner Solo Seat, single toe shifter, complete Ironsides chrome collection, flame levers, chrome brake cylinder covers, Windshield bag, saddle bag inserts.

text: Hi, My name is Jim and I just picked up a 2005 RK Custom with the carb, not fuel injected. All my bikes have had carbs. I know there is no reserve with the injected model. Did I make the right decision? Thanks!

author: JohnE
text: Injection has advantages like keeping the mixture right during altitude changes, no choke, fast starts. It doesn´t have that low, loping idle like the carbs have though. The idle needs to be up due to the fuel injection pressure. I went injection with both my Harleys w/ no regrets.

author: Johnhwise
text: Any Harley is the right decision.  Fuel injection is more effecient.  The carbs sound better.  Just keep the oil changed, 93 octane, and the service intervals up.  Ride safe my freind.  Welcome home!

author: vegimate
text: The weather shouldn´t be a problem. Fuel is high octane, consider availability. The tires are very good and do not grab at road cracks or road snakes (black tar filled grooves). Reliable steed. Check for leaking engine valve seals on the new bike. H-D put out 2 bad sets and got it right on the 3rd try but you have to find the problem - no recall. I have found it to be a good idea to invest in a battery tender to keep the battery at its peak. Just plug it in after a ride. Will allegedly extend battery life also.

text: I have put 3500 miles on my 05 Road King Classic this summer.  Love it.  Smooth with one or two up.  I, personally like to option of removable bags.  My last bike was a sportster.  It´s not nimble but the extra power makes up for it.  I wouldn´t part with it.  If you can do a demo ride or rent one that can help make your mind up. Good luck.

author: markfire
text: Hi I´m a UK potential Roadking buyer. I´ve been looking at the FLHRCi for a week now, but cant decide if i should buy one. Any plus or minus comments about this bike to help me make up my mind. regards Mark

author: Frank
text: After all these years, I finally test rode a RK today, and what a treat it was.  I am looking at a 99 FLHRCI.  I never fully appreciated what a great bike it is.  Appeared to be stock except for Tour Pack, after market pipes (no hi flow a/c), and a little more chrome (and in need of a complete cleaning and polish).  It ran great with good rubber at approx. 14500 miles on the clock. Are there any ghosts in the closet to be aware of? I am hoping that a goup of experts might be willing to aleart me to any cautions specific to the RK.  TIA and ride safe. Frank Baton Rouge

author: Hardy
text: New to discussion... I´m considering buying 2000 Road King and curious about responses to this posting.  I also want to know if fuel injected and if has automatic choke.

author: TW
text: I think the 99 models had a cam bearing problem but it was corrected by HD on later models. I am not sure of this maby sombody else would know.

author: wwo
text: I have a new 2005 Roadking Custom with Samson ovals for pipes. I never had 150 Km on it and the right side pipe was all blued. When I went to the dealer for my first servicing they changed the pipe. The mechanic told me that it will eventually blue on this side. It looked horrible. Now that I have a new pipe and its really blueing again and I only have 4000 KM on the bike. Is this normal or are these pipes no good.

author: James
text: I think it´s normal. Might want to buy some heat shields, cover up the blueing. My friend says if the bike is tune and running just PERFECTLY, the pipes won´t blue. Anyone have a perfectly tuned engine, with no sign of blue on the pipes? BTW, my stock pipes have blued a bit, in less than 1000 miles the dealer said they all do

author: Tim Steele
text: Greetings All I have had the privelege of owning my 95 FLHR for 2 years now .It has 20000 miles on it and I love it. I ride it 22 miles one way to wrk every day.it gets 38 MPG. I also collect 70s Vintage Kawasaki tripel 2 stroke street bikes. I am a retired USAF Flight Engineer (AFSOC )and I now work in a prison for the State.

author: Dan Murphy
text: Hello Tim, I am pretty vintage--was in the 1st Cav 69/70.  Have a 94 FLHR, 36k, gets about 45 to 46 mpg. Your seems a bit low on MPG? Does yours use any oil? You using 50weight? Been riding since 1965--had about everything but an Indian. Been with Harleys about 5 or 6 years--Road King is my favorite. Used to be in a Nam Vet Bike club but hung it up. Dan Murphy

author: Tim Steele
text: My bike has Bartels pipes and a screaming eagle ignition. I run Klotz Synthetic 20w50 and I ride 75-80 most of the time all highway to work and back. The main jets are richer than stock to acccomdate additional advance in the ignition and the less restrictive exhaust. Thanks, Tim Steele in Mossy Head FL

author: Tim Steele
text: I am having a front brake problem: The LH caliper is warmer than the right after running the bike down the road( without applying the brakes) . The pads are worn but not gone  and I just recently had a new front tire put on at the dealer ship. I disaasembled the LH caliper and found nothing wrong Reassembled  using factory service manuall . The wheel rolls free , but the lh disc is too hot to touch while the rh stays cool. I am going to pull it apart again and inspect the spacing and washers carefull. Anyone else had this ???. thanks

However I am afraid to turn a wrench on  either of them. I am always looking for friends that are able to lend a word of knowledge to help me out. LOL.

author: big ben
text: my 94 has oover 80 k and uses same amount i believe it blows by air intake also if i let it idle i will see a very small amount of blue smoke but i believe that is just where i gets past valves i was scared at first but hasnt gottin any worse and runs perfect have andrews cam sas carb sampson pipes it has never failed me once

author: Snowman
text: Hi everyone! Im new here (from Finland, so my english is not so good) and I bought a 1994 King. Haven´t thought about asking anything yet, maybe later. Nice to know it´s a forum just for a specific bike. :) Greetings Jan-E.

author: guy
text: I just took the engine guard off my 05 road king custom (i hate em). Im trying to find a set of highway pegs that are either frame mounted or floorboard mounted anybody have any answers???????

author: Dauntless
text: If anyone else is offended by the "billboard" mounting location of the license plate, sticking up in the air behind the seat, an answer is a stainless steel license plate relocation bracket offered by Soft Brake ($40).  It places the plate below the turn signal bracket.  Only two small problems....it is a real p***er to install and, if you are concerned, you have to find a new way to light the plate.  After installation I painted the top of my taillight lens flat black.  Results are much better looking than stock.  Why couldn´t Harley think of this?

author: slim pickens
text: I am borrowing a friends 2005 Roadking Classic.  Can anybody tell me what the red indicator light on the center console is?  I don´t want to ride it if its something that needs service.  it´s located at the bottom left hand side below the digital odometer. Thanks

author: jaimielan
text: That´s your cruise control light. On your left hand control there is a switch at the bottom which is your cruise. You can flip in off and the light will go away. On the right hand control is your set/accel/decel button

author: jaimielan
text: BTW, the light you should be concerned with is the little orange light right above it that looks like a gas pump. It is the low fuel indicator. I think you´ve got about a half a gallon when it comes on. Also, make sure the orange engine light goes off before you try to start it. Emjoy!

author: Bonjo
text: Does anyone know if cruise control was a std feature or just an option on the ´03 Road King Classic? People have told me both, and my dealer is giving me a "Classic" run-around...  Does anyone out there know for sure? Thanks! -Bonjo

author: Dauntless
text: Bonjo, cruise control was standard on the Road King Classic....or at least I didn´t pay anything extra and mine has it.  As I remember, the difference between the Classic and std. Road King was: wide whites, spoke wheels, leather bags, fuel injection and cruise were standard.  The alarm system was an extra.

author: Matt
text: Standard on my 03´ RK Classic Injected....not sure if the carb´d came with cruise.  Did they still have carbs available in 03?

author: Rob585
text: Cruise control is standard only on the the FLHRCI.

author: rdf24

author: young new rider
text: thinking of buying a new road king custom.  I have been riding for seven years,  always rode street bikes.  Need some advice,  I am 5 11 170 pounds,  Is the bike too heavy for me?  How does it handle at low mph.  And planning to ride it without saddle bags.  Can I chrome the shocks and struts?  and if so where?  I live in San Francisco.

author: rdf24
text: I bought the custom and couldnt be more pleased. Its a Bagger,but has a cruiser feel. At low speeds its like a Rock, good balance,can just about come to a complete stop without putting feet down.As for weight it is heavy but,but Im 5 9 and 150 LBS and I feel Comfortable. On the downside with my short legs saddle height is a bit much but still feet set flat on ground. I love Mine hope this helps

author: young new harley
text: thanks for the advice.  What do you think about removing the saddle bags and chroming the shocks and swing arm?  For a more rebel look! (with a single seat of course)

author: rdf24
text: Well to Start, The RKC was designed to run Bags, Trust Me they look good and you really need a place to put stuff. Though You might just want a Crusier not a Tourer,Which is what RKC is. So if yoi want to run Bags off, they sell already Chromed Shocks. Remember Without Bags the Support Frame will be seen, Dont look bad but the bags are part of what makes the RKC an RKC.

author: harryp
text: i harve 05 flhrsi i love it , it has the look i was looking for 25 yrs ago  and with  some changes here and there its my design

author: Rocky
text: I´m looking at a 2005 road kingFLHRSI with 50000 miles, this bike has 5 to 10 dollars of addons. well taken care of. any suggestions on what the bike is worth. is 50000 miles to many miles .Plus 5 to 6 thousand extra parts {new}.

text: I broke down and bought a new RK last spring, broke it in to 5K and then rode to Maggie Valley.......All is well and I think its the best bike I every had, (over twenty).  but....after it gets hot, the transmission shift sound like two guys fighting with dish pans...People tell me this in normal, HDs shift hard, but should the gear change be louder than the pipes???  comments please....

author: Greg DeMarco
text: I am a new owner of a 2001 Road King Injected. I bought the bike from Cycle COnnection in Little River SC. It runs great but no ownwes manual. I need to know capacities and pressure for the suspension system. I will buy a manuel when I can find one local. Anyone with an extra give me a mailing at gregwendy@hotmail.com. ()( Greg D.

author: Cathal
text: Ref air pressure. Max load you can pump up to 25#. DO NOT use a gas station air pump. Use a hand pump. You should use a syringe type with a gage built in.

author: Ron M.
text: Dr. Greg, I have a 2001 Road King and the air pressures for the front forks is 15psi and the rear shocks are 10psi. There is a sticker inside my saddlebags indicating the air pressure. Harley Davidson sells a pump for about $40 that does not allow air to escape while inflating. It is a high quality pump with a gauge.

author: rdf24
text: Dont know what is bad, Love mine. Granted mine is 2005, Ive put 5500 miles on it with no problems. If plan on putting a lot of miles like me (20000+ a Year) The service Plan is a must,Unlimted service for 4 Years

text: great bike the only negative thing i can say about it is that the beach style handlebars are a little uncomfortable. i swapped them out for a set of touring bars other than that my 05 is perfect

text: Good question. Mine are loose too! I tried to get to the clamps but couldn´t get to them.

text: Hello, I have a 1999 Road King that is 100% stock. Although it sounds ok, I think I would like just a little bit more noise. I hear differant stories. Some say it is ok to re mapp the computer with new exhaust pipes. Then, some tell of horror stories when doing this on the 1999 models because HD was still working out bugs with the fuel injection on the first year with the 88ci.  What can I do to get a little more noise without playing with the computer? Thank You, Chris Channon@sikorsky.com

author: lvnlg
text: Hi Chris, I have a 99 Road king Classic. Bought it new in 99. after I hit 1000 miles I replaced the stock mufflers with Sampson Fishtails and had the dealer do a Stage one Airflow and remap. I have never had any problems and the bike runs great. I have a buddy that has a 99 Road Glide Fule injected. he jsut wnt to a 95ci with Cams(Stage Ii) --no problems.

text: Hi Chris, I have a 99 Road king Classic. Bought it new in 99. after I hit 1000 miles I replaced the stock mufflers with Sampson Fishtails and had the dealer do a Stage one Airflow and remap. I have never had any problems and the bike runs great. I have a buddy that has a 99 Road Glide Fuel injected. he just went to a 95ci with Cams(Stage II) --no problems.

author: vegimate
text: You will have no weight issue with the Road King, it can carry the weight and the luggage very well.

author: BEN GRAVES
text: I HAVE BEEN A HARLEY OWNER FOR YEARS. OWNING A SPORTSTER, SUPERGLIDE, and HERITAGE.  I HAVE BEEN RIDING MY NEW ROAD KING NOW FOR 13 MONTHS AND HERE ARE MY THOUGHTS. 1. MOST COMFORTABLE BIKE I HAVE OWNED FOR LONG RIDES. 2. PACKING CAPACITY IS UNBELIEVABLE. 3. TWO UP RIDING IS EASY AND COMFORTABLE. 4. FUN FUN FUN 5. FUEL INJECTION IS THE ONLY WAY TO GO. 6. HARDLEY ANY VIBRATION AT CRUISING SPEEDS )70MPH I WILL BE SUPRISED IF I EVER BUY ANOTHER KIND OF HARLEY.

text: I HAVE BEEN A HARLEY OWNER FOR YEARS. OWNING A SPORTSTER, SUPERGLIDE, and HERITAGE.  I HAVE BEEN RIDING MY NEW ROAD KING NOW FOR 13 MONTHS AND HERE ARE MY THOUGHTS. 1. author: joe
text: greatbike love it

author: Bruce
text: hey there, y´all!

author: John Zufall
text: Just bought 05 RK, as my first venture into the harley "family", I admit I might not be the best one to rate this machine. But here goes. 1. On the standard, the first thing I found I really needed to do was change the handlebars, the stock ones had me leaning over the tank to much for comfort on a long ride. So I replaced with bars from wild-1 that are similiar to stock on the custom, much better. 2. This is no doubt the biggest bike I have owned(last bike BMW R1200C) and will take a little getting used to, but the power is unmistakable, and of course the looks,feel and sound are amazing. 3. Not sure yet if I will lower and give up some comfort for a little lower wheel base, maybe. That is all I have for now, as here in Indiana its cold,snowing, and not much good for riding right now. John Z

author: phil
text: hello,I currently dropped a deposit on a 2005 FLHRI Road King and I had the same feeling when I sat on it in the showroom, the bars leaned me over a little too far, I was wondering if the beach bars would help,HD told me the stock bars could be turned down toward me.But after reading your review I feel my 1st impression was correct,Now I can look for a pair of beach bars and throw them on when I get the bike,Can I ask how tall you are? maybe the height has something to do with the lean over the tank,I may be a little too short for the stock bars,Thanks for the info.

author: phil
text: to answer: I installed pullback bars from an 04 road king custom along with a seat from an 05 road king custom....this is one comfortable ride now...no more reaching,

author: Philip
text: My RK is overheating w/blueing pipes and melting reflectors.  I have Screaming Eagle mufflers and intake.  Harley-Davidson of the Woodlands charged nearly $200.00 to install the download Harley stated I needed with the Screaming Eagle Parts. I believe either the download was done incorrectly or ??.  It is also popping back through the mufflers and the intake with the exhaust reaching over 550F on the outside of the header pipes.  Any help out there?

author: Tony Mortillaro
text: What was the model # of the handle bar you purchased from Wild-1?

author: Dereke Mims
text: The Road King Standard is my third Harley-Davidson motorcycle. I have only been riding for about a year and a half now, and Harley’s are the only motorcycles I have owned. The Road King is for me, by far the best touring/curser made. I also changed the handlebars out for ones that allow me to set back on long road trips. The only real issue I have with the bike is the stock seat, and I plan on changing it out with a Mustang seat with back rest. The stock seat is too wide up front which does not allow me to place my feet squarely on the road at stops. Other than that the Road King is a great bike for motoring around town or touring down I45 to Houston. Ride Safe my Brothers and Sisters!

author: GEORGE
text: SPARK PLUGS NEED CHANGING AFTER ABOUT 500-1000 MILES,FRONT CYLINDER PLUG IS ALWAYS BLACK , THE BIKE ALWAYS RUNS RUFF . EVEN TRIED SCREAMING EAGLE PLUGS AND IT RAN WORSE.IT HAS BEEN LIKE THIS FOR AWHILE AND I TAKE IT TO THE DEALER FOR ALL IT´S SERVICE LAST ONE WAS 50,000 MILE , NOW HAVE 53,OOO AND HAVE CHANGED THE PLUGS FOUR TIMES SINCE THEN.

author: J.D.
text: Hello, What dshould the stock lb setting be for a 03 FLHRCI?  And what would you suggest it to be for a two up ride (Total weight 300 lbs)

author: Dauntless
text: As per an unreliable dealership (aren´t they all?) the stock pressure is 7#.  I have installed the kit to lay the shocks down a bit and found that aggressive riding with 7# on bumpy roads would bottom out and that 10-12# feels better.  With two up I would try 10-12 and see how it feels.

author: benji756
text: I run 10 psi for single and 15 psi for 2 riders

author: Dan Kennelly
text: First, I´m no Harley mechanic, but what I have found on mine is that close to 20# helps handling with one up and 20 to 25 should keep you from bottoming out with a passenger.

author: Reloader
text: I have just purchased an "05" RKC I am having the dealer install 95" Big Bore Stage 2 kit with flat top pistons 203 cam and Screaming Eagle Air Filter. I am installing Rinehart True Dual exhaust with a Dynojet Power Commander. Does anyone know of a map for this setup. I was told by power commander tech support that they did not have any maps for the "05" Touring Bikes. Will the "04" maps work with the "05" bikes? Thanks, Chris

author: Gubs
text: I have just purchased a low mileage ´96 Road King and am thinking about changing the bars to either the Heritage or Wide Glide bars. I am leaned a little to far forward with the stock bars and find it to much of a stretch. Has anyone done this? Will I have to add to the wiring harness? Etc. Any help or comments would be greatly appreciated. Thanks!

author: Studley
text: I bought a used 96 with Heritage bars already on it. My wife´s 04 had them changed to Heritage bars. Much better than stock for the reason you mentioned. No wiring or cable changes. Well worth it.

author: Craig
text: I am thinking of updating my tank on a ´98 RoadKing Classic, FI.  I found a factory tank from a 2001 Road King Classic, fuel injected.  Would I have any issues installing that tank on my 1998? Thanks,

author: Matt
text: I´d like to add a docking windshield and passing lamps to my 04 RK Custom.  I´ve found a windshield on EBAY, but it didn´t come with any hardware.  Anyone done this yet? Thanks

author: Mike
text: You probably can get the black rubber bushings and spacers necessary to mount the windshield at your local HD dealer. I don´t remember if the bolts that hold the bushings and spacers need to be longer than stock to accomodate the bushings.  Passing lamp brackets also use same mounting bolts.

author: Lee
text: The dealer installed a removable windshield and passing lamps on my R K Custom   I love the windshield at highway speeds on long trips . I arrive feeling less fatigued.  I remove it for local cruising because after all, we ride to be in the wind.

author: Joe Plazek
text: Put the Harley detacthable one on and its great. Have a look at www.plazek.com and click on "Motorcycles" joe.

author: Matt
text: I´d like to add a docking windshield and passing lamps to my 04 RK Custom.  I´ve found a windshield on EBAY, but it didn´t come with any hardware.  Anyone done this yet? Thanks

author: Matt
text: My ´new´ 04 RK Custom has an annoying ´howl´ when you do a light to average braking.  This is coming from the front wheel.  It builds-up.  Like an oscilation.  It is quiet when you do a hard braking.  It stops fine - but it´s annoying.  I drove an 04 demo (w/300 miles) that did the same thing.  When my bike arrived and it did it, the dealer said it would go away.  Well, 3200 miles later it´s still doing it.  I´m thinking it has something to do with the style of wheel.  Are other RK Custom owners experiencing the same thing? Also, regarding the front brake, has anyone noticed a scraping sound when you aren´t using the brake at all?  I haven´t replaced the stock pipes, so it probably won´t be noticed when that happens, but still another annoying factor.  Again, my dealer (and two others) have said it will go away with miles.  :( Love the bike.  Want to do a bunch of things to it. ($$)

author: Jerry
text: My 04 RK Custom with 2,000 + miles has the ´howl´, not necessarly when braking.  I often shut the motor off coming down my driveway so as not to stir up to much noise and I hear what I think is the brake pad slightly rubbing the disc.  A great bike.

author: Mike
text: Matt, I have 6K on my ´04 RKC.  I have not heard anything on the front brake other than the normal pad/rotor contact.  I have noticed both front and rear rotors being slightly warped, occasionally causing the pads and the rotor to make brief contact on each rotation.  Only real brake problem I have is the rear brake doesn´t have much stopping ability beyond just the single rotor and the weight transfer during braking.  Instead of the normal 70/30 bias front to back, mine is more 90/10.

author: Joe Plazek
text: Yep,,, mine too! Brakes make the bike sound like a piece od shit in the city. Don´t know. Will change the pads in the spring and I will assume it disapears. joe.

author: gary
text: My brakes have been making the holwing noise since my first trip home from the shop. The brakes also make the scraping sound when just runing along with out the brakes "engaged". I found that if I pull the brake handle just a little, it goes away. I wrote a letter to Harley and they said "sorry". I have seen several other web sites that indicate that it is a caliper sticking problem. Went to the dealer and they said that I should get up to highway speed and squeeze the front brake until it is just short of locking up. YEA RIGHT! I gues they figured they already got my money, so they don´t need me around any more. Since they don´t want to help me, I am just using the noise as a means of letting other potential harley customers know about the quality of service that harley provides for their customers. So far, I know of three people that bought other brands of bikes after hearing my brakes.

author: SMC
text: Yes, My RKC - 2005 does the same annoying thing. Its reall apparent in traffic and people frown when they hear the sound. My dealership keeps telling me its going to go away, but thats BS too. I have a 1000 miles on it and if anything its getting louder. I´m lost atthis point and don´t know where to turn too.

author: matt
text: Just turned 5k on it.  Still does it.  Guess I´ve learned to live with it.  A friend actually ran next to the bike and placed his fingers on the rotor and could mute the howl somewhat.  Dealers have been no help at all.

author: Pat
text: Boy sounds familiar. At least I know I´m not the only one. I have an ´05 w/ same thing. Dealership is trying to help but no luck yet. Been through 3 different sets of pads so far w/ no cure. Love the bike otherwise.

author: Lee

author: The west coaster
text: Try to check the vent on your tank.  A plugged vent cap can lead to vapor lock.

author: Dogbertx
text: A quicker test would be to just loosen your filler cap. See if there is a release of air pressure. A buddies bike had the same problem. The temporary fix wasn´t cooler gas but the act of opening the tank (which you have to do to put the gas in) which released the pressure. As above, then check to see if your vent is plugged. Dogbertx, Canada

author: Mike
text: I had my ´04 RK Custom for about 5500 miles.  Recently, I switched to HD synthetic and noticed slightly less vibration at speed. I´ve ridden 500 miles since.  I have not noticed any additional engine noise.  Valve ticking at freeway speeds is about where it was with non-syn oil. I have heard it is not recommended to switch back to non-syn.  I don´t know the reason why however.  I do know that my BMW automobile really liked Mobil 1 and I noticed a significant improvement.  I ran Mobil 1 for 50,000 miles with virtually no compression or oil loss. But that´s not a applicable motorcycle issue, being air-cooled.

author: Chas Robertson
text: What do you want to know?  I have had an ´02 Classic now for almost two years.  I have put about 5000 miles on it and about 5 million hours playing with it.  I have ripped the front end completely apart (the nacelle is poorly chromed from the factory, expect it to flake apart), and spent wonderful hours with a laptop and a power commander remapping the throttle curves to match the intake and exhaust add ons.  To prospective purchasers...first, let the baby breathe.  Absolute MUST is to put a stage I intake and a Power Commander on.  I think the factory pipes actually did a better dyno job, but some Screamin´ Eagle or V&H will be louder and cannot hurt.  Then have it set by a dyno shop.  Double check the laser alignment (probably a waste of $100, but with a bike this heavy, turn dymanics are critical).  Expect to go through tires.  Serious cases of "chrome-itis" abound after a while and you get an incredible urge to chrome every damn thing on the bike...but indulge yourself, it is great therapy.  Bike is probably pricier than some of its competition...but nothing in the world like the admiring glances of passers by, or the sound of the big V-Twin rumbling through the north Georgia mountains.  Incredibly safe highway tourer.  Not a great choice for bumper to bumper Atlanta rush hour commutes in July and August (no friggin a/c), but surprisingly nimble and low center of gravity without the heavy fairings and tour packs.  There are good reasons for getting other bikes, but I am nearly 50, and I have never heard anyone wistfully say "Thats nice but, my whole life I always wanted a Yamaha".  Also, I find it interesting that the vast majority of Pacific Rim cruisers all have gone to a lot of trouble to look like a Harley...go figure.

author: Shore King
text: Ive owned my King since July and have 2000 miles on it. I have added python3 slip-ons and had the fuel injection remapped and so far the bike has been great. There haven´t been any mechanical problems so far. The balance of the bike is great for such a large machine. The acceleration is fine for me. I don´t see any need for the 95" upgrade at this time. It´s great for 2-up riding although the stock seat gets on your nerves after a few hours. I added Heritage Softail bars and that relieved a lot of stress on the lower back. I´m 6´2 and 270 lbs and the stock bars made me reach a bit, but the new bars are fabulous. It wasn´t necessary to replace any of the stock hoses to add the bars. It was close, but it is fine. The stock shield is too high as well. I have added an aftermarket shield and that helps a lot. Price-wise, all Harleys are overpriced, but I don´t regret the purchase at all. If you are in the market for one of these hot rods, then go for it. I do have the squealing front brake syndrome as many others have, but its ok to me. It isn´t too intrusive. There is a lot of mechanical noise from the drivetrain to the valves in the heads. I dont care so much because I like to think of it as a hot rod from the 60s with solid lifters and all the appeal of the tire-fryers from back then. They could have added all the cam driven goodies of today but then you wouldnt have the bike that they build. Harley has a lot of things that come with it...like inflated prices on all the extra parts, but I accepted that when I paid for the bike. Its a done deal. I love the bike and would do it again in a second. Anyone who says this bike runs like junk, obviously didnt do any research because this is no crotch rocket and was never intended to be. This is a serious road machine for watching the mile roll past. Questions or opinions welcome.

author: Woody
text: Shore King:  I have owned many bikes (non-Harley).  One and a half years ago I bought a Sportster just for the single man´s thrill of it (even though I am married, but the wife said she wasn´t interested in riding).  Well, in two years I have taken her many places and she has decided she absolutely LOVES to ride.  It´s the ride and all the comradery that goes with it at the end of the road.  After 11,000 mileson the Sportster, a month ago, for her comfort, I bought a 2003 Road King (new one for $14,000).  :-)  I live in Korea (U.S. Army), and the bike is on the high seas now; I can´t wait til it gets here.  Thanks for the words you wrote about your own experience with your Road King (mine is gunmetal pearl also, by the way); I hope I have the same experience you have with it -- PURE PLEASURE!!!  Hope to meet you out on the road one day.  By the way, where do you live?  Woody  :-)

author: Griff
text: Hi guys (& gals?) I´m partially retired and live in Switzerland. I´m a novice motorbike rider (only previous experience: three weeks on rentals) though I was a fairly avid road cyclist at one time. Last Monday (six days ago now), I took delivery of a new W800, and it´s even better´n I´d imagined. It´s a blast running it up the pass roads that I used to do years ago on the cycle -- and very soon I´ll be past the first run-in phase, which means that I can pile on a few more revs. As of this writing, I have managed to keep the dirty side down for almost 1000 km. Knock on wood (Ow!) that it´ll stay that way. However, there are a couple of things about the stock bike that don´t help. First (and this is hearsay), the BRAKES. For a German reviewer (experienced tuner Ulf Penner), they didn´t pass muster. He recommended replacing the OEM brake line with stainless steel braided line; and the OEM pads with the ones Kawasaki uses in the GPZ 1100. Sounded good to me, and the dealer installed the SS brake line before I picked up the bike; he was fresh out of the pads, though. Result: no complaints, even without the pads. Braking seems as crisp as with the rental bike (BMW F650GS). If there´s any news worth telling after the pads are installed next week, I´ll let you know. However, there´s an unexpected issue with the rear TIRE. Even after run-in, the rear end fishtails a bit, or floats, when it encounters a lengthwise unevenness in the asphalt: a joint, for example, or even a pavement marking. The bike does not feel solid at those moments, and though the rear may not break out, it can turn me into a real ´fraidy-cat when it happens going into a downhill corner. Well, so try the simple stuff first, right? The dealer put 36 PSI into the rear, the correct value for the upper load range (above 215 lbs). But at 200 lbs, I´m still in the low-load range, and dropping the pressure accordingly to 32 PSI (only 4 PSI!) brought considerable improvement ... ... though it didn´t solve the problem. What´s up with this thing? Could the deep center groove in the tread be \

author: Hills
text: I have always used fully synthetic two stroke oil in my RS. The power valve was cleaned around 8-900 miles ago and it´s started clogging already – the aprilia dealer suggested it should last between 2-4000 miles before needing cleaning. I don´t ride it full throttle all the time but I´m not riding with it at 3 thousand all day. Every trip gets the revs up to 11 thousand at least once; do you have to ride it like an %&#!¤-hole to stop it clogging; could the power valve be faulty? Could riding it with a clogged power valve have damaged it in anyway? Took it on motorway for a couple of miles with the needle between 9 & 10 thousand but if anything it made it worse. At the moment it performs like my old yamaha tzr 50 but if you force it up to the power band (takes ages in present condition) it will force the valve open and it will perform almost like it would normally. The throttle is like an on/off switch with a long delay feature. The bike is rideable but I´m not riding it until it goes in for power valve cleaning tomorrow, trust me it will be 15 miles of hell with white van man able to out accelerate a 33 bhp (more like 5) rs125. If anyone has any suggestions I´d be glad to hear them. Thanks.

text: Jose, check and see if your CDi bridge has been cut. It´s a small black box under your rear seat. It might have a dip in the ignition timing for environmental purposes. EU bikes all have this. Cut the metallic bridge in it (after cutting through the silicon) and that should sort it out.

author: Kevin Cogge
text: If you want a more reliable moped you have to take the DNA (it´s a four stroke and those are more reliable than the two stroke). Aprilia rs 125 is a two stroke (so less reliable) but the top speed lies way beyond where the DNA will ever go. The newest rs 125 also has plastic wheels in the engine so if they break --) engine doesn´t get enough oil --) get´s stuck. It´s really a choice between reliable but slow (+- 60 mph) or fast (could get up to 100 mph) but not one of the most reliable bikes.

author: RAGHU
text: Its really superb bike in India especially for long tour or ride. I have Avenger, its EKS (Engine Kill Switch) is fitted by company itself. Its really superb bike, I have never seen such comfort and performance in any other indian bike. Basically I love the feature of Low height, length of the bike, superb seating, comfort, 55watt headlight, back rest and finally engine. I have finished 4 free service (just last week of august07). I have not got any problem till now. I have done Teflon Coating too.

text: Hi guys, I have bought avenger 3 months back and have been facing 2 problems, first one is that the shocks is not taking the impact when you go over a uneven road or even a small ditch. I have not found this problem with any other bike i rode till now and even the bike skids very easily(note : I have alligned the wheels many times by the dealer) I am scared to go into muddy road even at 10kmph and no question of goin into a wet soil..it just drifts like the new movie tokyo drift....Can anyone advise..and can I change the shockers..Can we fit enfields new gas shock absorber to avenger..and can anything be done about the rear seat...to make it wider and comfortable. Waiting for your comments Lucky

author: crazy_yogi
text: TO RIDE SMOOTH ON THE SHOCHS KEEP THE SETTING OF THE SHOCKS AT THE LOWEST LEVEL AND ON THE ROUGH RODE DRIVE CAREFULLY AND U WILL FIND THE DIFFERENCE, AND FOR THE DRIFTNESS AND THE ALLIGNMENT CHANGE IT TO ALLOY-WHEELS WHICH GIVES THE ULTIMATE KILLING LOOK AT THE BIKE, MARK MY WORDS, REGARDS CRAZY YOGI
